title: Flat Topological Nodal Lines in Heavy-Fermion Compound CeCoGe$_3$
summary: The interplay between strong electronic correlations, unconventional superconductivity, and symmetry-protected topology provides a fertile ground for discovering exotic quantum states. In this work, we investigate the correlated electronic structure and topological properties of the heavy fermion material CeCoGe$_3$ using density functional theory combined with dynamical mean-field theory calculations. Our results reveal a crossover from high temperature incoherent states to low temperature coherent heavy quasiparticles, accompanied by a mass enhancement of $m^*/m_{\text{DFT}}\sim 52.6$ at $T=25$ K. The interplay between electronic correlation, spin-orbit coupling and the noncentrosymmetric $I4mm$ crystal symmetry stabilize flat topological nodal lines within 10 meV of the Fermi level, which could contribute a significant density of states. The proximity of topological nodal lines to the Fermi surface suggests a potential role in mediating pressure induced unconventional superconductivity. Our work establishes CeCoGe$_3$ as a prototype topological nodal line Kondo semimetal. The coexistence of strong correlation, non-trivial band topology and superconductivity indicate CeCoGe$_3$ as a potential candidate for realizing topological superconductivity.

AI.tldr: This study investigates CeCoGe3 as a heavy fermion material using DFT+DMFT calculations. It reveals a crossover to coherent heavy quasiparticles with mass enhancement ~52.6, and identifies flat topological nodal lines near the Fermi level stabilized by spin-orbit coupling and noncentrosymmetric I4mm symmetry. The material is proposed as a prototype topological nodal line Kondo semimetal with potential for topological superconductivity.
AI.motivation: The interplay between strong electronic correlations, unconventional superconductivity, and symmetry-protected topology provides a fertile ground for discovering exotic quantum states. The goal was to investigate the correlated electronic structure and topological properties of the heavy fermion material CeCoGe3.
AI.method: Density functional theory (DFT) combined with dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) calculations were used to investigate the correlated electronic structure and topological properties of CeCoGe3.
AI.result: A crossover from high-temperature incoherent states to low-temperature coherent heavy quasiparticles was revealed, accompanied by a mass enhancement of m*/m_DFT ~ 52.6 at T=25 K. The interplay between electronic correlation, spin-orbit coupling, and the noncentrosymmetric I4mm crystal symmetry stabilizes flat topological nodal lines within 10 meV of the Fermi level.
AI.conclusion:
CeCoGe3 is established as a prototype topological nodal line Kondo semimetal. The coexistence of strong correlation, non-trivial band topology, and superconductivity makes it a potential candidate for realizing topological superconductivity.

The study reveals a crossover from high-temperature incoherent states to low-temperature coherent heavy quasiparticles, with significant mass enhancement. Flat topological nodal lines near the Fermi level could contribute substantial density of states. The proximity of these nodal lines to the Fermi surface suggests they may play a role in mediating pressure-induced unconventional superconductivity.

title: Coherent-state ansatz for the Holstein polaron in one and two dimensions
summary: The Holstein model often serves as an archetype for electron-phonon interactions and polaron formation in solids. However, precise descriptions of the Holstein polaron are difficult when the phonon frequency is small and the electron-phonon coupling is strong, due to the presence of many phonons in the ground state. We present a semi-analytical approximation that consists of a variational ansatz with clouds of phonons surrounding the electron in the form of coherent states. This becomes particularly simple and exact in the Lang-Firsov limit. We determine the domain of validity away from this limit, and further explore the improvement achieved with a removal of the requirement that the phonon clouds form coherent states. Both approximations work extremely well at strong coupling, and both work surprisingly well also at weak coupling. The coherent-state ansatz provides a simple and intuitive picture of the polaron ground-state wavefunction, and in addition predicts accurate values for the ground-state energy and effective mass.

AI.tldr: A semi-analytical variational approach using coherent-state phonon clouds is developed to describe Holstein polarons, particularly in the challenging strong-coupling, small-phonon-frequency regime.
AI.motivation: The Holstein model is a fundamental archetype for electron-phonon interactions, but precise descriptions fail when phonon frequency is small and coupling is strong due to the large number of phonons in the ground state.
AI.method: A variational ansatz with phonon clouds in the form of coherent states, which becomes exact in the Lang-Firsov limit. The approach is extended by removing the coherent-state requirement to improve accuracy.
AI.result: Both approximations work extremely well at strong coupling and surprisingly well at weak coupling, providing accurate predictions for ground-state energy and effective mass.
AI.conclusion: The coherent-state ansatz offers a simple, intuitive physical picture of the polaron ground-state wavefunction while maintaining high numerical accuracy.

title: Alleviating Projection-Space Sensitivity in DFT+U via Renormalized U
summary: Although the DFT+U method significantly improves the description of correlated electronic systems, its accuracy is known to depend strongly on the input parameters including local projection space used for the Hubbard correction. As a result, calculations performed with different projection sizes can yield quantitatively different -- and sometimes divergent -- results. In this work, we investigate the dependence of the effective Coulomb interaction $U_{\mathrm{eff}}$ on projection size using constrained DFT calculations for rutile TiO$_2$ and $β$-MnO$_2$. We find that as the projection size increases, the self-consistently calculated $U_{\mathrm{eff}}$ decreases significantly -- by as much as $33$\%. This trend is attributed to renormalization of the Coulomb interaction through orbital relaxation and enhanced screening. When $U_{\mathrm{eff}}$ values recalculated for each projection size are employed, the results for lattice parameters, electronic structure, and relative phase stability become consistent across different projection sizes. These findings can provide a practical route to alleviate projection-size dependence in DFT+U calculations.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how the effective Coulomb interaction (U_eff) in DFT+U calculations depends on the projection size used for Hubbard correction, finding that U_eff decreases by up to 33% as projection size increases due to orbital relaxation and screening effects.
AI.motivation: DFT+U method improves description of correlated electronic systems but produces quantitatively different and sometimes divergent results depending on local projection space used for Hubbard correction, creating inconsistency across calculations.
AI.method: Constrained DFT calculations were performed on rutile TiO2 and β-MnO2 to investigate U_eff dependence on projection size.
AI.result: U_eff decreases significantly (up to 33%) as projection size increases due to renormalization of Coulomb interaction through orbital relaxation and enhanced screening. When U_eff values are recalculated for each projection size, results become consistent across different projection sizes for lattice parameters, electronic structure, and relative phase stability.
AI.conclusion: Recalculating U_eff for each projection size provides a practical solution to alleviate projection-size dependence in DFT+U calculations, ensuring reliable and consistent results.

title: Phase field as a front propagation method for modeling grain growth in additive manufacturing
summary: A mesoscopic grain-envelope model applying a phase-field front-propagation method is developed to simulate grain growth under additive manufacturing process conditions. The envelope represents the outer surface of dendritic grains through a diffuse interface. While a modified heat-conduction model that incorporates moving heat sources and latent-heat release provides the evolution of local thermal field. Envelope propagation is determined from microscopic-solvability-based kinetic law. The model is validated through two- and three-dimensional simulations and subsequently applied to examine the influence of material and process parameters on microstructure evolution. The results demonstrate that the proposed mesoscopic model offers an efficient and predictive approach for modeling grain growth during multi-pass and multi-layer build-up in additive manufacturing.

AI.tldr: A mesoscopic grain-envelope model with phase-field front-propagation is developed to simulate grain growth during additive manufacturing, using a modified heat-conduction model with moving heat sources and latent-heat release, validated via 2D/3D simulations.
AI.motivation: Simulate grain growth under additive manufacturing process conditions, which is challenging due to complex thermal fields from moving heat sources and repeated melting-solidification cycles in multi-pass/multi-layer builds.
AI.method: Phase-field front-propagation method with grain envelope representing dendritic grain outer surfaces via diffuse interfaces; modified heat-conduction model incorporating moving heat sources and latent-heat release; microscopic-solvability-based kinetic law for envelope propagation.
AI.result: Model validated through 2D and 3D simulations; successfully examines influence of material and process parameters on microstructure evolution; offers efficient and predictive approach for multi-pass and multi-layer additive manufacturing builds.
AI.conclusion: The mesoscopic grain-envelope model provides an efficient and predictive framework for modeling grain growth in additive manufacturing, applicable to complex multi-layer build-up processes.

title: Quasiparticle spectroscopy in tantalum films with different Ta/sapphire interfaces
summary: One of the crucial aspects of current research in quantum information science is the identification and control of loss mechanisms in superconducting circuits. Although microwave measurements directly quantify device performance, additional techniques that probe quasiparticle excitations in superconducting films are needed to understand the microscopic mechanisms underlying dissipation and decoherence. Here, we present results from quasiparticle spectroscopy of Ta/sapphire films by measuring the Meissner-state magnetic susceptibility using a precision frequency-domain resonator specifically designed for thin films. We find direct evidence for additional low-energy excitations in samples with lower internal quality factors. These excitations are consistent with deep subgap states due to two-level systems, Yu-Shiba-Rusinov states near the gap edge, and perhaps other pair-breaking mechanisms. The developed non-destructive frequency-domain quasiparticle spectroscopy is a valuable addition to the quantum materials toolbox.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a non-destructive frequency-domain quasiparticle spectroscopy technique using Meissner-state magnetic susceptibility measurements to identify loss mechanisms in superconducting Ta/sapphire films, revealing low-energy excitations (two-level systems, Yu-Shiba-Rusinov states) that correlate with reduced internal quality factors.
AI.motivation: Microwave measurements alone cannot fully quantify device performance; additional techniques are needed to probe quasiparticle excitations and understand the microscopic mechanisms underlying dissipation and decoherence in superconducting circuits.
AI.method: Quasiparticle spectroscopy of Ta/sapphire films by measuring the Meissner-state magnetic susceptibility using a precision frequency-domain resonator specifically designed for thin films.
AI.result: Direct evidence was found for additional low-energy excitations in samples with lower internal quality factors. These excitations are consistent with deep subgap states due to two-level systems, Yu-Shiba-Rusinov states near the gap edge, and possibly other pair-breaking mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: The developed non-destructive frequency-domain quasiparticle spectroscopy is a valuable addition to the quantum materials toolbox for understanding dissipation and decoherence mechanisms in superconducting circuits.

title: Qubit discretizations of d=3 conformal field theories
summary: We propose that scaling dimensions of d=3 conformal field theories can be studied on a system of qubits with near term quantum simulation platforms. Our proposal chooses couplings of quantum many-body problems on a polyhedral lattice at which the conformal state-operator correspondence can be observed most accurately in the spectrum. We validate our protocol on the Ising model where we extract the scaling dimensions of a number of scaling operators with a few percent accuracy from the spectrum of a system of just 20 qubits. The procedure makes only minor assumptions beyond general conformal invariance -- it may hence be applied widely. Requirements and challenges to realize this proposal on quantum computers are discussed. Our results demonstrate that for current or near term qubit platforms, three dimensional conformal field theories present a unique opportunity -- a forefront problem that is difficult on classical computers but may be solved through quantum simulation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using near-term quantum computers to study scaling dimensions of 3D conformal field theories by simulating quantum many-body systems on polyhedral lattices. The method was validated on the Ising model, achieving a few percent accuracy with just 20 qubits.
AI.motivation: Three-dimensional conformal field theories (CFTs) are important in theoretical physics but difficult to study with classical computers. The authors propose that quantum simulation platforms could potentially solve this problem, as quantum computers are naturally suited for simulating quantum many-body systems.
AI.method: The proposal involves mapping CFT problems onto qubits using a polyhedral lattice geometry, where the conformal state-operator correspondence can be observed in the spectrum. The method makes minimal assumptions beyond general conformal invariance, making it widely applicable. Validation was performed on the Ising model.
AI.result: Successfully extracted scaling dimensions of several scaling operators with a few percent accuracy from the spectrum of only 20 qubits. This demonstrates the feasibility of the approach for near-term quantum devices.
AI.conclusion: Three-dimensional CFTs represent a promising application for current and near-term quantum computers - a problem that is classically challenging but may be solvable through quantum simulation. The paper discusses requirements and challenges for implementing this on actual quantum hardware.

title: Collapse of Jahn-Teller Phonons in La$_{1-x}$Sr$_{x}$MnO$_3$ with Weak Magnetoresistance
summary: Perovskite manganites are quantum materials exhibiting competing interactions inducing colossal magnetoresistance (CMR). The prevailing theory of CMR highlights the essential role of electron-phonon coupling (EPC), but mounting evidence suggests the underlying mechanism is more complicated. Here, we investigate phonons and spin-phonon coupling in ferromagnetic CMR manganites La$_{1-x}$Sr$_x$MnO$_3$ ($x$=0.2,0.3) with relatively small CMR associated with melting of the magnetic order above room temperature. High-resolution neutron scattering experiments combined with density functional theory (DFT) show that the low-temperature ferromagnetic phase is conventional: neutron scattering from phonons agrees with DFT predictions and magnons follow sinusoidal dispersions. Fluctuating magnetic moments and low-energy phonons remain conventional in the high temperature paramagnetic phase, indicating the Mn and La/Sr sublattices are not strongly perturbed by melting of ferromagnetism. In contrast, the Jahn-Teller-active optical oxygen vibrations collapse entirely above the Curie temperature, despite low CMR in these compositions, with some of the lost spectral weight reappearing as quasielastic scattering. We attribute this highly anomalous behavior to giant EPC in the charge and/or orbital channel. It drives cooperative diffusive motion of quasistatic carrier-trapping oxygen sublattice distortions once ferromagnetism disappears. We hypothesize the magnitude of magnetoresistance correlates with the rate of diffusion rather than with the strength of Jahn-Teller EPC.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates phonons and spin-phonon coupling in ferromagnetic CMR manganites La1-xSrxMnO3 (x=0.2, 0.3) using neutron scattering and DFT, finding that Jahn-Teller-active optical oxygen vibrations collapse above the Curie temperature, suggesting magnetoresistance correlates with diffusion rate rather than JT EPC strength.
AI.motivation: While electron-phonon coupling (EPC) is considered essential for colossal magnetoresistance (CMR), evidence suggests the mechanism is more complex. This study aims to understand the role of phonons and spin-phonon coupling in CMR manganites, particularly in compositions with relatively small CMR.
AI.method: High-resolution neutron scattering experiments combined with density functional theory (DFT) were used to investigate La1-xSrxMnO3 (x=0.2, 0.3) across ferromagnetic and paramagnetic phases.
AI.result: The low-temperature ferromagnetic phase is conventional - neutron scattering from phonons agrees with DFT predictions and magnons show sinusoidal dispersions. Both fluctuating magnetic moments and low-energy phonons remain conventional in the high-temperature paramagnetic phase. However, Jahn-Teller-active optical oxygen vibrations collapse entirely above the Curie temperature, with some spectral weight reappearing as quasielastic scattering. This anomalous behavior is attributed to giant EPC in the charge and/or orbital channel, driving cooperative diffusive motion of quasistatic carrier-trapping oxygen sublattice distortions.
AI.conclusion: The magnitude of magnetoresistance correlates with the rate of diffusion rather than with the strength of Jahn-Teller EPC. The Mn and La/Sr sublattices remain unperturbed by melting of ferromagnetism, indicating the mechanism involves subtle oxygen sublattice dynamics.

title: Capturing nuclear quantum effects in high-pressure superconducting hydrides and ice with nuclear-electronic orbital theory
summary: Nuclear quantum effects are essential for correctly describing hydrogen-rich materials at high pressures. Superconducting hydrides and ice are prime examples of such systems, requiring the inclusion of lattice anharmonicity and nuclear quantum effects to correctly predict and describe the structures and phase transition pressures observed experimentally. Herein, we show that the nuclear-electronic orbital density functional theory (NEO-DFT) method, which treats specified nuclei quantum mechanically on the same level as the electrons, is capable of accurately describing nuclear quantum effects in superconducting hydrides and ice. NEO-DFT predicts the hydrogen-bond symmetrization pressure in H$_3$S and D$_3$S, benchmarking against the more expensive stochastic self-consistent harmonic approximation (SSCHA) method, and predicts the correct symmetric Fm$\bar{3}$m structure for LaH$_{10}$ at a wide range of pressures. NEO-DFT also predicts the ice VIII to ice X phase transition pressures for H$_2$O and D$_2$O in agreement with experimental measurements. The accuracy, computational efficiency, and broad applicability of the NEO method opens the door for expanded large-scale studies into these types of systems.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that NEO-DFT can accurately describe nuclear quantum effects in superconducting hydrides (H3S, D3S, LaH10) and ice (H2O, D2O) at high pressures, predicting phase transitions and structures in agreement with experiments and more expensive methods, while offering improved computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Nuclear quantum effects are essential for correctly describing hydrogen-rich materials at high pressures, but current methods are either inaccurate or computationally expensive. There is a need for an accurate yet efficient method to predict structures and phase transition pressures in superconducting hydrides and ice.
AI.method: Nuclear-electronic orbital density functional theory (NEO-DFT), which treats specified nuclei quantum mechanically on the same level as electrons, incorporating lattice anharmonicity and nuclear quantum effects.
AI.result: NEO-DFT accurately predicts hydrogen-bond symmetrization pressure in H3S and D3S (benchmarking against SSCHA), correct symmetric Fm-3m structure for LaH10 across wide pressure range, and ice VIII to ice X phase transition pressures for H2O and D2O in agreement with experimental measurements.
AI.conclusion: NEO-DFT is an accurate, computationally efficient method for describing nuclear quantum effects in hydrogen-rich materials at high pressures. It successfully predicts hydrogen-bond symmetrization in H3S/D3S, the correct structure for LaH10, and ice phase transition pressures for H2O/D2O in agreement with experiments. This method enables large-scale studies of such systems.

title: ETHER: An Efficient Tool for Monte Carlo Simulations of Magnetic Systems
summary: Monte Carlo (MC) simulations are powerful computational tools for investigating thermodynamic behavior and validating analytical approaches in complex physical systems. Here we present ETHER (Efficient Tool for THermodynamics Exploration via Relaxations), an open-source MC simulation package, developed for studying temperature-dependent magnetic properties in spin systems. ETHER enables large-scale MC simulations of various spin systems to analyze phase transitions, critical behavior, and complex magnetic structures. The package constructs spin-lattice networks from standard structural input files and supports exchange interaction definitions through user-specified neighbor lists. It also provides tools for easy visualization and post-processing of simulation outputs. Our code has been benchmarked thoroughly against results reported in the literature for common representative magnetic systems. This user-friendly code offers researchers a versatile platform for exploring thermodynamic properties of complex magnetic systems.

AI.tldr: ETHER is an open-source Monte Carlo simulation package for studying temperature-dependent magnetic properties in spin systems, enabling large-scale simulations of phase transitions and critical behavior with visualization tools.
AI.motivation: Need for efficient computational tools to investigate thermodynamic behavior and validate analytical approaches in complex physical systems, specifically temperature-dependent magnetic properties in spin systems.
AI.method: Monte Carlo simulations using spin-lattice networks constructed from structural input files with user-specified neighbor lists for exchange interactions; benchmarked against literature results.
AI.result: ETHER enables large-scale MC simulations for analyzing phase transitions, critical behavior, and complex magnetic structures, with easy visualization and post-processing tools.
AI.conclusion: ETHER provides a user-friendly, versatile platform for exploring thermodynamic properties of complex magnetic systems, with potential for broad application in magnetic materials research.

title: Effect of Exchange-Correlation Functionals on Schottky Barriers at Si/Metal Interfaces
summary: Accurate prediction of Schottky barrier heights (SBHs) at metal-semiconductor (M-SC) interfaces is essential for understanding and optimizing charge injection in electronic and optoelectronic devices. However, first-principles calculations of SBHs remain challenging due to the combined difficulties of semiconductor bandgap underestimation, metal Fermi level placement, lattice-mismatch, relative geometric alignment and electrostatic potential alignment across heterogeneous interfaces. In this work, we present a systematic and physically grounded assessment of computational strategies for SBH prediction using Si(111)/Metal (Al, Cu, Ag, Au) interfaces as representative test cases. We evaluate multiple exchange-correlation (XC) treatments, in combination with three distinct bulk reference protocols: relaxed bulk, relaxed bulk with spin-orbit coupling, and strained bulk references consistent with the interface geometry. By benchmarking against experimental data, we demonstrate that structural and electrostatic consistency between interface and bulk reference calculations is the dominant factor governing SBH accuracy. We show that mixed hybrid-semilocal approaches combined with strained reference protocols yield uniformly positive and significantly improved SBHs, achieving near-experimental accuracy while maintaining a favorable balance between computational cost and predictive performance. Our results establish a clear and transferable methodology for reliable Schottky barrier prediction and provide practical guidance for large-scale screening and interface engineering.

AI.tldr: This work presents a systematic assessment of computational strategies for predicting Schottky barrier heights (SBHs) at metal-semiconductor interfaces, using Si(111)/Metal (Al, Cu, Ag, Au) as test cases. The study finds that structural and electrostatic consistency between interface and bulk reference calculations is the dominant factor for SBH accuracy, and that mixed hybrid-semilocal approaches combined with strained reference protocols achieve near-experimental accuracy.
AI.motivation: Accurate prediction of Schottky barrier heights at metal-semiconductor interfaces is essential for understanding and optimizing charge injection in electronic and optoelectronic devices. However, first-principles SBH calculations remain challenging due to combined difficulties including semiconductor bandgap underestimation, metal Fermi level placement, lattice-mismatch, relative geometric alignment, and electrostatic potential alignment across heterogeneous interfaces.
AI.method: The authors evaluated multiple exchange-correlation (XC) treatments in combination with three distinct bulk reference protocols: relaxed bulk, relaxed bulk with spin-orbit coupling, and strained bulk references consistent with interface geometry. They used Si(111)/Metal (Al, Cu, Ag, Au) interfaces as representative test cases and benchmarked against experimental data.
AI.result: The study demonstrates that structural and electrostatic consistency between interface and bulk reference calculations is the dominant factor governing SBH accuracy. Mixed hybrid-semilocal approaches combined with strained reference protocols yield uniformly positive and significantly improved SBHs, achieving near-experimental accuracy while maintaining a favorable balance between computational cost and predictive performance.
AI.conclusion: The results establish a clear and transferable methodology for reliable Schottky barrier prediction and provide practical guidance for large-scale screening and interface engineering in electronic and optoelectronic device development.

title: Higgs gap modes in superconducting circuit quantisation
summary: We extend a recently developed projective circuit quantisation approach to incorporate superconducting Higgs modes associated to gap dynamics. This approach starts from a microscopic fermionic Hamiltonian for mesoscopic superconductors, and projects the system onto its low-energy "BCS" Hilbert space. We derive analytical results for the superconducting Higgs mass, "spring" constant, and oscillation frequency of the gap dynamics, which we validate numerically. We compute anharmonic corrections to the Higgs frequency for higher excitations of small superconducting islands, and compare our results to previous long-wavelength calculations.

AI.tldr: This paper extends a projective circuit quantization approach to incorporate superconducting Higgs modes associated with gap dynamics, deriving analytical formulas for Higgs mass, spring constant, and oscillation frequency from a microscopic fermionic Hamiltonian, with numerical validation and anharmonic corrections for small superconducting islands.
AI.motivation: The research addresses the need to accurately describe superconducting Higgs modes (collective amplitude oscillations of the order parameter) from a quantum mechanical perspective, particularly for mesoscopic systems where previous long-wavelength approximations may be insufficient. The Higgs mode is a fundamental excitation in superconductors related to the amplitude mode of the order parameter, and this work aims to provide a more complete quantum treatment starting from microscopic principles.
AI.method: The approach starts from a microscopic fermionic Hamiltonian for mesoscopic superconductors and projects the system onto its low-energy BCS Hilbert space using projective circuit quantization. Analytical derivations are performed for key quantities (Higgs mass, spring constant, oscillation frequency), followed by numerical validation. Anharmonic corrections are computed for higher excitations of small superconducting islands, and results are compared to previous long-wavelength calculations.
AI.result: The paper presents analytical results for the superconducting Higgs mass, "spring" constant, and oscillation frequency of gap dynamics, validated numerically. It computes anharmonic corrections to the Higgs frequency for higher excitations of small superconducting islands and compares these results to previous long-wavelength calculations, showing good agreement in appropriate limits while providing more detailed quantum mechanical insights.
AI.conclusion: The projective circuit quantization approach successfully incorporates superconducting Higgs modes and provides accurate analytical descriptions of gap dynamics from microscopic principles. The work bridges the gap between microscopic theory and macroscopic phenomena, demonstrating that the approach captures both the fundamental Higgs mode and anharmonic corrections relevant for small superconducting systems, with results consistent with previous calculations in appropriate limits.

title: Spin Group Symmetry Criteria For Unconventional Magnetism
summary: Unconventional magnetism has typically been classified into two fundamental classes: even-parity magnets (EPMs) and odd-parity magnets (OPMs). These two classes exhibit identical and opposite spin splittings, respectively, under momentum inversion, while both maintain symmetry-compensated magnetization. In this Letter, we present a unified spin space group-based framework that establishes comprehensive symmetry criteria for both classes. Our framework not only yields a complete classification of EPMs and OPMs but also uncovers a wealth of new symmetry-driven mechanisms for them. Specifically, we classify both classes into three types based on their spin textures: collinear (type-I), coplanar (type-II), and noncoplanar (type-III), and we demonstrate that both classes can be realized across collinear, coplanar, and noncoplanar magnetic orders. We identify eight distinct symmetry-driven mechanisms for OPMs and seven for EPMs, among which some paradigms of unconventional magnetism, for instance, altermagnets naturally emerge as one specific mechanism of EPMs. Using these established criteria, we identify numerous candidate materials from the Magndata database, realizing some new symmetry mechanisms for OPMs and EPMs. Our work establishes a foundational symmetry framework for understanding, predicting, and designing unconventional magnetic materials.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a unified spin space group framework for classifying unconventional magnets (EPMs and OPMs) into three types based on spin textures, identifying 8 mechanisms for OPMs and 7 for EPMs, with altermagnets being one example of EPMs.
AI.motivation: Unconventional magnetism has been classified into two fundamental classes (EPMs and OPMs) with different spin splittings under momentum inversion, but a unified comprehensive symmetry criteria framework was lacking.
AI.method: A unified spin space group-based framework that establishes comprehensive symmetry criteria for both even-parity magnets (EPMs) and odd-parity magnets (OPMs), classifying them into three types based on spin textures (collinear type-I, coplanar type-II, and noncoplanar type-III).
AI.result: Identified eight distinct symmetry-driven mechanisms for OPMs and seven for EPMs, with altermagnets emerging as one specific mechanism of EPMs. Numerous candidate materials were identified from the Magndata database.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a foundational symmetry framework for understanding, predicting, and designing unconventional magnetic materials, and identifies candidate materials from the Magndatabase.

title: Patterns of load, elastic energy and damage in network models of architected composite materials
summary: We investigate the role of architected thin films in the interfacial failure properties of bi-layer composites. Our results show that, while graded structures can be used to prescribe failure at the interface, they do not offer significant advantages in terms of fracture toughness. Hierarchically patterned layers can localize failure at the interface and simultaneously enhance interface toughness, by enforcing a buffer region where elastic energy is dissipated in the form of diffuse damage, so that no stress concentration can drive crack growth. To analyze these mechanisms, the associated patterns of local load redistribution and the soft deformation modes, we develop a network formalism that brings together concepts of discrete differential geometry and spectral graph theory.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates architected thin films for controlling interfacial failure in bi-layer composites, showing that hierarchically patterned layers outperform graded structures by both localizing failure at the interface and enhancing toughness through energy-dissipating buffer regions.
AI.motivation: Understanding and controlling interfacial failure in bi-layer composites is crucial for engineering applications. While graded structures can direct failure location, it remains unclear how to simultaneously achieve failure localization and improved fracture toughness at the interface.
AI.method: The authors develop a network formalism that integrates concepts from discrete differential geometry and spectral graph theory to analyze patterns of local load redistribution and soft deformation modes in the composite structures.
AI.result: Graded structures can prescribe failure at the interface but provide no significant improvement in fracture toughness. In contrast, hierarchically patterned layers successfully localize failure at the interface while enhancing interface toughness by creating a buffer region where elastic energy dissipates as diffuse damage, preventing stress concentration from driving crack growth.
AI.conclusion: The network formalism combining discrete differential geometry and spectral graph theory effectively captures the mechanisms of load redistribution and deformation in architected thin films, demonstrating that hierarchical patterning is a superior design strategy for interface engineering in composite materials.

title: Interband pairing in two-band superconductors with spin-orbit and Zeeman couplings
summary: Interband pairing in multiband superconductors is often neglected because of its higher energetic cost compared with intraband pairing. We show that, in multiband systems, a Zeeman magnetic field can stabilize interband pairing through the near degeneracy of spin-split branches from different bands, even within a minimal on-site attractive interaction. Using hexagonal tight-binding models with locally broken inversion symmetry, we find a Zeeman-driven transition between a conventional intraband s-wave state and an interband-dominated superconducting Mixing state. The resulting quasiparticle spectrum is intrinsically gapless, leading to anomalous thermodynamic behavior, including a T-linear specific heat at low temperatures, reflecting a finite zero-energy density of states.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that Zeeman magnetic fields can stabilize interband pairing in multiband superconductors through spin-split branch degeneracy, leading to a gapless "Mixing" state with anomalous T-linear specific heat behavior.
AI.motivation: Interband pairing is typically neglected in multiband superconductors due to its higher energetic cost compared to intraband pairing. This work challenges that assumption by showing that Zeeman magnetic fields can stabilize interband pairing through near degeneracy of spin-split branches from different bands, even with minimal on-site attractive interaction.
AI.method: The authors employed hexagonal tight-binding models with locally broken inversion symmetry to investigate the problem.
AI.result: A Zeeman-driven transition was discovered between conventional intraband s-wave state and an interband-dominated superconducting "Mixing" state. The resulting quasiparticle spectrum is intrinsically gapless, leading to anomalous thermodynamic behavior including T-linear specific heat at low temperatures, reflecting a finite zero-energy density of states.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals that Zeeman fields can stabilize interband pairing in multiband superconductors, leading to a transition from conventional intraband s-wave to an interband-dominated "Mixing" state. This state exhibits intrinsically gapless quasiparticle spectrum with finite zero-energy DOS, resulting in anomalous thermodynamic behavior including T-linear specific heat at low temperatures.

title: Weyl excitonic condensation
summary: We consider a half-filled two-dimensional Su-Schrieffer-Heeger lattice and examine the role of the long-range Coulomb electron-hole attractive interaction. We demonstrate that, under specific conditions, a rare interplay of topological and excitonic-collective behavior emerges as a novel state of matter. A unique Bose-Einstein condensate of excitons forms, exhibiting co-presence of pseudo-spin chiral texture. The emerging complex order-parameter, a particle-hole pairing-gap, has non-zero real and imaginary parts throughout the Brillouin zone (BZ) but vanish separately on two different nodal lines, which intersect at two Weyl points. The Weyl nodes possess opposite pseudo-spin chiralities, which act as source and drain of a Berry-flux associated with the particle-hole pairing-wavefunction, and are the cause of Bogoliubov-deGennes Fermi-arc edge-states. We self-consistently calculate the full momentum-dependence of the particle-hole pairing gap throughout the entire BZ. Near the Weyl points, the pairing gap exhibits the unconventional time-reversal-symmetry breaking $p_x+ip_y$ character. Finally, we discuss general potential experimental realizations of this novel state of matter.

AI.tldr: This theoretical work predicts a novel topological exciton condensate in a 2D SSH lattice with electron-hole interactions, featuring complex order parameters, Weyl points with opposite chiralities, and Fermi-arc edge states.
AI.motivation: The study aims to explore the interplay between topological phases and electron-hole interactions in a 2D Su-Schrieffer-Heeger lattice. The authors investigate what happens when long-range Coulomb attractive interactions lead to exciton formation in a system that already possesses topological properties.
AI.method: The researchers perform self-consistent calculations of the momentum-dependent particle-hole pairing gap throughout the entire Brillouin zone. They analyze the nodal structure, Weyl points, pseudo-spin chirality, and Berry flux properties of the resulting state.
AI.result: They discover a novel state of matter where Bose-Einstein condensed excitons coexist with topological characteristics. The order parameter (particle-hole pairing gap) has complex values with non-zero real and imaginary parts throughout the BZ. Two nodal lines intersect at two Weyl points with opposite pseudo-spin chiralities. These Weyl nodes act as source and drain of Berry flux and give rise to Fermi-arc edge states. Near Weyl points, the gap exhibits p_x+ip_y symmetry.
AI.conclusion: This work reveals a rare combination of excitonic condensation and topological physics, potentially realizable in experimental systems involving layered 2D materials or heterostructures where both topological order and electron-hole interactions can be tuned.

title: Bridging the lab-to-fab gap in non-fullerene organic solar cells via gravure printing
summary: Organic solar cells have reached record efficiencies with non-fullerene acceptors, yet their translation to industrial printing remains a critical bottleneck. Here we report the highest efficiency achieved for a fully roll-to-roll-compatible gravure-printed non-fullerene organic solar cell. High-performance blends are typically optimised under laboratory coating conditions, while roll-to-roll manufacturing imposes fundamentally different constraints on ink stability, drying dynamics, and multilayer integration. Whether these constraints intrinsically limit device physics has remained unresolved. Here, we demonstrate a gravure-printed PM6:Y12 solar cell architecture using commercially available materials and establish a quantitative framework that disentangles optical, recombination, and transport losses in printed devices. We find that favourable bulk morphology and exciton harvesting can be preserved under gravure printing and non-halogenated solvents. The dominant efficiency penalties arise instead from optical interference within the printed layer stack and slow charge transport. Our results demonstrate that the performance gap between laboratory and printed solar cells is originating from device architecture rather than the intrinsic physics of modern non-fullerene systems, providing a mechanistic roadmap for roll-to-roll manufacturing of non-fullerene solar cells.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the highest efficiency for fully roll-to-roll-compatible gravure-printed non-fullerene organic solar cells using PM6:Y12. The study establishes a quantitative framework to analyze losses in printed devices, finding that morphology can be preserved but the main limitations are optical interference and slow charge transport, indicating the performance gap stems from device architecture rather than intrinsic material physics.
AI.motivation: Organic solar cells with non-fullerene acceptors have achieved high efficiencies in laboratory settings, but translating these to industrial roll-to-roll printing remains a critical bottleneck. It was unclear whether manufacturing constraints intrinsically limit device physics.
AI.method: The study used PM6:Y12 solar cell architecture with commercially available materials, employing a quantitative framework to disentangle optical, recombination, and transport losses in printed devices. Gravure printing with non-halogenated solvents was demonstrated.
AI.result: Favourable bulk morphology and exciton harvesting can be preserved under gravure printing and non-halogenated solvents. The dominant efficiency penalties arise from optical interference within the printed layer stack and slow charge transport, rather than morphology issues.
AI.conclusion: The performance gap between laboratory and printed solar cells originates from device architecture rather than intrinsic physics of modern non-fullerene systems. This provides a mechanistic roadmap for roll-to-roll manufacturing of non-fullerene solar cells.

title: Signatures of Topological Superconductivity and Josephson Diode Effects on the Magnetocurrent-Phase Relation of Planar Josephson Junctions
summary: We present a theoretical study of proximitized planar Josephson junctions (JJs) with Rashba spin-orbit coupling (SOC) subject to an in-plane magnetic field and demonstrate that the magneto-current-phase relation (magneto-CPR) provides a powerful and unified probe of their microscopic and topological properties. By analyzing the full phase and Zeeman-field dependence of the supercurrent, we show that magneto-CPR measurements allow one to reconstruct the ground-state phase that minimizes the system's free energy in the absence of phase bias. This reconstructed phase generally displays 0-pi-like transitions as a function of the Zeeman energy, and we demonstrate that the magnitudes of the associated phase jumps provide quantitative information about the Rashba SOC. We further show that the mixed phase-field response encoded in the magneto-CPR enables the extraction of the second mixed spin susceptibility, which serves as a sensitive diagnostic of gap closings and can be used to construct a superconducting topological phase diagram in terms of the relative topological gap. In addition, the magneto-CPR yields the field dependence of the forward and reverse critical currents, allowing one to characterize the Josephson diode effect and its connection to the Zeeman field, Rashba SOC, and junction transparency. Our results establish magneto-CPR measurements as a versatile spectroscopic tool that can be used to extract key system parameters and provide evidence of topological superconducting phases in planar JJs.

title: Fractional Topological Phases, Flat Bands, and Robust Edge States on Finite Cyclic Graphs via Single-Coin Split-Step Quantum Walks
summary: We report the first realization of a fractional topological phase in a fully unitary, noninteracting discrete-time quantum walk implemented on finite cyclic graphs. Using a single-coin split-step cyclic quantum walk (SCSS-CQW), we uncover topological phenomena that are inaccessible within conventional cyclic quantum-walk dynamics. The protocol enables controlled engineering of quasienergy spectra, flat bands, and topological phase transitions through the step-dependency parameter and coin-rotation angle. We show that cyclic graphs with even and odd numbers of sites exhibit qualitatively different band structures, while rotational flat bands arise exclusively in $4n$-site cycles; a general analytic condition for their emergence is derived. The SCSS-CQW produces fractional winding numbers $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ (Zak phases $\pm \fracπ{2}$), in sharp contrast with the integer invariants of standard quantum walks. These fractional invariants lead to an unconventional bulk--boundary correspondence and support edge states beyond the usual integer topological classification. In the step-dependent protocol, transitions between distinct fractional winding sectors generate robust edge modes. Numerical simulations show that these states remain stable in the presence of both dynamic and static coin disorder as well as phase-preserving perturbations, while survival-probability analysis demonstrates their long-time persistence. Requiring only a constant number of detectors independent of the evolution time, the proposed scheme offers a minimal-resource and experimentally accessible platform for realizing fractional topology, flat bands, and protected edge states in small-scale synthetic quantum systems.

AI.tldr: First realization of fractional topological phases in unitary, noninteracting discrete-time quantum walks on finite cyclic graphs using a single-coin split-step cyclic quantum walk (SCSS-CQW), demonstrating fractional winding numbers ±1/2, flat bands, and robust edge states.
AI.motivation: Conventional cyclic quantum-walk dynamics cannot access certain topological phenomena; the study aims to engineer controllable quasienergy spectra, flat bands, and topological phase transitions through step-dependent parameters.
AI.method: Implemented a single-coin split-step cyclic quantum walk (SCSS-CQW) with step-dependency parameter and coin-rotation angle to engineer quasienergy spectra; used numerical simulations to test stability against dynamic/static coin disorder and phase-preserving perturbations.
AI.result: Discovered fractional winding numbers ±1/2 (Zak phases ±π/2) instead of integer invariants; found that 4n-site cycles produce rotational flat bands; demonstrated unconventional bulk-boundary correspondence and robust edge modes persisting at long times.
AI.conclusion: The SCSS-CQW provides a minimal-resource, experimentally accessible platform for realizing fractional topology, flat bands, and protected edge states in small-scale synthetic quantum systems, requiring only a constant number of detectors independent of evolution time.

title: AIMD-L: An automated laboratory for high-throughput characterization of structural materials for extreme environments
summary:
Rapid developments in artificial intelligence and machine learning as applied to materials science are creating an urgent need for experimental data, which can be provided by high-throughput and autonomous laboratories. To date most demonstrations of such laboratories have focused on functional materials, with less attention paid to structural materials. We present here the Artificial Intelligence in Materials Design Laboratory (AIMD-L), an automated, high-throughput facility for characterizing the microstructure and properties of structural metals and ceramics, with an emphasis on materials in extreme environments.
  AIMD-L has two custom instruments for characterization of structural materials: HELIX for shock studies of materials, and MAXIMA for X-ray diffraction and X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy. Specifically designed for high-throughput studies, HELIX and MAXIMA are each capable of collecting data at rates two to three orders of magnitude faster than conventional systems. A third experimental station, SPHINX, is a commercial nanoindenter modified for integration into the automated workflow of AIMD-L. A user (which may be human or an AI agent) directs the experiments to be carried out by means of a centralized control program. The experimental stations are linked by a conveyance that moves samples around the lab, with a robot at each station for sample transfer in/out of the instrument. The experimental stations also communicate with a common data layer that streams data autonomously from each instrument to a data portal, where their arrival triggers automated workflows for data reduction and analysis. The processed data are immediately available to the human operator or agentic AI, forming a closed loop for rapid decision-making and experimental control.

AI.tldr: AIMD-L is an automated, high-throughput laboratory for characterizing microstructure and properties of structural metals and ceramics, featuring custom instruments (HELIX for shock studies, MAXIMA for X-ray spectroscopy) and integrated nanoindentation, with autonomous data streaming and AI-ready closed-loop control.
AI.motivation: The urgent need for experimental data in materials science driven by AI/ML advancements, with most prior automated labs focusing on functional materials while structural materials received less attention.
AI.method: Three custom/experimental stations (HELIX, MAXIMA, SPHINX) connected by robotic conveyance system, controlled by centralized program, with common data layer streaming to portal triggering automated reduction/analysis workflows.
AI.result: Two custom instruments achieve data collection rates 2-3 orders of magnitude faster than conventional systems; closed-loop system enables rapid decision-making for human operators or AI agents.
AI.conclusion: AIMD-L demonstrates a fully integrated automated laboratory for structural materials characterization, enabling high-throughput experimentation with autonomous data processing suitable for AI-driven materials discovery.

title: Enhancement of metallicity by Na doping in La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_{7+δ}$
summary: The observation of high-$T_c$ superconductivity in bilayer nickelate La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ under high pressure provides a new venue for exploring novel unconventional superconductors and elucidating the mechanism of high-$T_c$ superconductivity. Subsequently, numerous chemical substitution studies have been reported, aiming to stabilize superconductivity at ambient pressure, or significantly reduce the pressure threshold required for its occurrence. Here, we report the comprehensive study on sodium (Na) doping in the Ruddlesden-Popper nickelate La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_{7+δ}$, where Na$^+$ substitutes for La$^{3+}$ at the A-site with varying doping concentrations. The structural, thermal, magnetic, and electronic transport properties of as-synthesized polycrystalline samples were systematically investigated. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis reveals that Na doping induces a structural transition from the '327' Amam phase to the '4310' Bmab phase when $x\geq0.075$, which is further corroborated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) measurements. Substitution of La$^{3+}$ with Na$^+$ gives rise to a gradual expansion of the '327' phase lattice. Meanwhile, resistivity measurements indicate that the density wave (DW) transition is marginally suppressed and metallicity is significantly enhanced. Upon the application of pressure, DW transition can be further suppressed, whereas the low-$T$ insulating behaviors remain insensitive to pressure. These results offer critical insights into the roles of elemental substitution and charge carrier doping in steering the competing electronic phases in layered nickelates.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates sodium (Na) doping effects in the Ruddlesden-Popper nickelate La3Ni2O7+δ to explore ways of stabilizing superconductivity at lower pressures. Through XRD, TGA, and resistivity measurements, researchers found that Na doping triggers a structural transition at x≥0.075, expands the lattice, suppresses the density wave transition, and enhances metallicity, providing insights into controlling competing electronic phases in layered nickelates.
AI.motivation: The discovery of high-Tc superconductivity in bilayer nickelate La3Ni2O7 under high pressure opened new avenues for exploring unconventional superconductors. However, achieving superconductivity typically requires high pressure conditions. This study aimed to stabilize superconductivity at ambient pressure or significantly reduce the pressure threshold through chemical substitution, specifically sodium (Na) doping at the La site.
AI.method: The researchers synthesized polycrystalline samples of Na-doped La3Ni2O7+δ with varying Na concentrations. They performed systematic investigations including: (1) X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis to examine structural changes, (2) Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) to study thermal properties, (3) Resistivity measurements under various pressures to investigate electronic transport properties, and (4) Characterization of magnetic properties.
AI.result: Key findings include: (1) Na doping induces a structural phase transition from the '327' Amam phase to the '4310' Bmab phase when x≥0.075; (2) Substitution of La3+ with Na+ causes gradual expansion of the '327' phase lattice; (3) The density wave (DW) transition is marginally suppressed while metallicity is significantly enhanced; (4) Application of pressure further suppresses the DW transition; (5) Low-temperature insulating behaviors remain insensitive to pressure.
AI.conclusion: These results offer critical insights into the roles of elemental substitution and charge carrier doping in steering the competing electronic phases in layered nickelates. The study demonstrates that Na doping can effectively modulate the structural and electronic properties of La3Ni2O7+δ, providing valuable guidance for future efforts in stabilizing superconductivity at ambient conditions in nickelate superconductors.

title: Magnetic and electrical transport properties of the single-crystalline half-Heusler antiferromagnet DyNiSb
summary: High-quality single crystals of the half-Heusler compound DyNiSb were investigated for their low temperature thermodynamic and magnetotransport properties. Magnetic susceptibility, heat capacity, and electrical resistivity measurements revealed two distinct magnetic phase transitions at TN1 = 7.3 K and TN2 = 3.4 K, contrasting with previous reports on polycrystalline samples, which identified only a single transition near TN2 . Moreover, the studied samples were found to exhibit Metal like conductivity, at odds with a semiconducting behavior reported for the polycrystals. Magnetoresistance measurements performed in both transverse and longitudinal configurations revealed in small magnetic fields a weak antilocalization effect that diminishes with increasing temperature, giving way to a positive, monotonic magnetoresistance at high temperatures. Angular-dependent resistivity studies showed a crossover from fourfold to twofold symmetry with increasing magnetic-field strength, suggesting a field-induced reconstruction of the Fermi surface. Our findings highlight a complex magnetic and electrical transport behavior in DyNiSb, highly sensitive to structural disorder and easily tunable by external magnetic field.

AI.tldr: High-quality single crystals of the half-Heusler compound DyNiSb were investigated, revealing two magnetic phase transitions (7.3K and 3.4K) instead of one, metallic conductivity instead of semiconducting behavior, weak antilocalization effect at low fields, and a crossover from fourfold to twofold symmetry with increasing magnetic field strength.
AI.motivation: Previous reports on polycrystalline DyNiSb showed only a single magnetic transition near 3.4K and semiconducting behavior, prompting investigation of single crystals to clarify the true physical properties and understand the discrepancies.
AI.method: Magnetic susceptibility, heat capacity, and electrical resistivity measurements at low temperatures, along with magnetoresistance studies in transverse and longitudinal configurations, and angular-dependent resistivity measurements.
AI.result: Two distinct magnetic phase transitions at TN1 = 7.3 K and TN2 = 3.4 K were identified. Samples showed metallic conductivity. Magnetoresistance revealed weak antilocalization at low fields (diminishing with temperature) and positive monotonic magnetoresistance at high temperatures. Angular-dependent studies showed crossover from fourfold to twofold symmetry with increasing field strength.
AI.conclusion: DyNiSb exhibits complex magnetic and electrical transport behavior highly sensitive to structural disorder and easily tunable by external magnetic field, with field-induced reconstruction of the Fermi surface.

title: Adsorption-Controlled Epitaxy and Twin Control of $γ$-GaSe on GaAs (111)B
summary: The III-Se layered semiconductors, including InSe and GaSe, are promising optoelectronic materials due to their relatively high electron mobilities at room temperature, nonlinear optical responses, ferroelectricity, self-passivated van der Waals surfaces, and ability to alloy and synthesize heterostructures for bandgap engineering. Adsorption control is a widely utilized strategy for controlling the stoichiometry and phase formation of these materials; however, the bounds of the adsorption-controlled growth window for GaSe have not been systematically established. Additionally, challenges with control over polytype and twinning remain. Here, we use molecular beam epitaxy to experimentally map the adsorption-controlled growth window of GaSe films on vicinal GaAs (111)B substrates. The observed phase boundaries show qualitative agreement with Ellingham diagram predictions. All films crystallize in the $γ$ ($R3m$) polytype. Increasing growth and annealing temperature leads to decreased mosaicity measured by x-ray rocking curve and smoother surfaces measured by atomic force microscopy, at the expense of a transition from singly oriented $γ$ to twinned $γ$ with $60\degree$ rotated domains.

AI.tldr: This paper maps the adsorption-controlled growth window of GaSe thin films on GaAs (111)B substrates using molecular beam epitaxy, establishing phase boundaries that align with Ellingham diagram predictions while revealing a temperature-dependent tradeoff between film quality and twinning.
AI.motivation: III-Se layered semiconductors like GaSe are promising for optoelectronics, but the adsorption-controlled growth window for GaSe had not been systematically established, and challenges with polytype and twinning control remained unresolved.
AI.method: Molecular beam epitaxy was used to grow GaSe films on vicinal GaAs (111)B substrates, systematically mapping the adsorption-controlled growth window. X-ray rocking curves and atomic force microscopy were used to characterize film quality.
AI.result: The phase boundaries show qualitative agreement with Ellingham diagram predictions. All films crystallize in the γ (R3m) polytype. Increasing growth and annealing temperature reduces mosaicity and produces smoother surfaces, but induces a transition from singly oriented γ to twinned γ with 60° rotated domains.
AI.conclusion: The adsorption-controlled growth window for GaSe has been experimentally mapped and validated against thermodynamic predictions. A fundamental tradeoff exists between film quality (low mosaicity, smooth surfaces) and structural perfection (avoiding twinning), which must be balanced for optimal device applications.

title: On the estimating the superconducting volume fraction from the internal magnetic susceptibility
summary: Zhang et al.$^1$ reported zero-field cooled (ZFC) and field cooled (FC) data measured in a highly compressed $Pr_4Ni_3O_{10}$ single crystal. These measurements provide unambiguous confirmation of bulk superconductivity in pressurized Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates. Zhang et al.$^1$ applied an equation (described in Refs.$^{2,3}$) to recalculate ZFC data measured in a $Pr_4Ni_3O_{10}$ (sample S3) in volume fraction $f$ of the superconducting phase in the sample. In result$^1$, $f = 0.85$ was reported for sample S3 at pressure P = 40.2 GPa. The key postulate of the methodology for the calculation of $f$ (see also works$^{4-6}$) is that $f$ is equal to the amplitude of the internal magnetic susceptibility $\vert χ_{internal} \vert $, or $ f = \vert χ_{internal} \vert $. Here we argue that this postulate is incorrect and present counterexample where the $Pr_4Ni_3O_{10}$ sample S3 can exhibit $f < 0.10$ and $\vert χ_{internal} \vert = 0.82 $. In the result, we addressed recent Replies$^{2,3}$ on our Comments$^{7,8}$. Considering that the postulate $ f = \vert χ_{internal} \vert $ is widely used in superconductivity, we extend our request to reconsider the validity of this postulate in the entire field of superconductivity.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that the commonly used postulate f = |χ_internal| for calculating superconducting volume fraction is incorrect, using Pr_4Ni_3O_{10} as a counterexample where f < 0.10 but |χ_internal| = 0.82. The authors request reconsideration of this methodology's validity across the superconductivity field.
AI.motivation: The authors argue that the key postulate used to calculate superconducting volume fraction (f = |χ_internal|) is incorrect. This methodology is widely used in superconductivity research, so its invalidity has broad implications for the field.
AI.method: The authors analyze the methodology used in previous studies (Refs. 1-3) for calculating superconducting volume fraction f. They present a counterexample where the calculated f value differs dramatically from |χ_internal|. The paper addresses replies to their previous comments (Refs. 7,8).
AI.result: The authors present a counterexample showing f < 0.10 while |χ_internal| = 0.82 in Pr_4Ni_3O_{10} sample S3 at pressure P = 40.2 GPa, demonstrating a significant discrepancy between the volume fraction and magnetic susceptibility.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that the postulate f = |χ_internal| is incorrect and should be reconsidered in the entire field of superconductivity. They demonstrate with a counterexample that f < 0.10 while |χ_internal| = 0.82 in Pr_4Ni_3O_{10} sample S3.

title: Anomalous magnetotransport in the single-crystalline half-Heusler antiferromagnet ErPdSb
summary: We report the thermodynamic and magnetotransport properties of the half-Heusler antimonide ErPdSb, studied on single-crystalline samples in wide ranges of temperature and magnetic fields. The compound was found to order antiferromagnetically at 1.2 K. In the paramagnetic state, it shows semimetallic behavior with a broad hump in the temperature-dependent electrical resistivity around 70 K. The results of ab initio calculations of the electronic structure of ErPdSb indicated a bulk insulating nature. In small magnetic fields the magnetoresistance is driven by a weak antilocalization effect, while in strong fields it is negative and describable by the deGennes-Friedel formalism. The Hall effect data indicated that holes are the dominant charge carriers. At 2 K, the Hall conductivity exhibits a sizable anomalous contribution, which is obscured by multiband effects at higher temperatures. The angular magnetoresistance shows unusual features as functions of magnetic field and temperature, pointing to a possible field-induced reconstruction of the Fermi surface.

AI.tldr: Study of ErPdSb half-Heusler compound reveals antiferromagnetic ordering at 1.2 K, semimetallic behavior with complex magnetoresistance effects (weak antilocalization and negative MR), hole-dominated transport, and signatures of field-induced Fermi surface changes
AI.motivation: Investigation of thermodynamic and magnetotransport properties of the half-Heusler antimonide ErPdSb to understand its electronic structure, magnetic ordering, and transport behavior
AI.method: Single-crystalline sample growth, electrical resistivity, magnetoresistance, Hall effect, and angular magnetoresistance measurements across wide temperature and magnetic field ranges, combined with ab initio electronic structure calculations
AI.result: ErPdSb exhibits antiferromagnetic ordering at 1.2 K, semimetallic behavior with resistivity hump at 70 K, bulk insulating nature from calculations, weak antilocalization magnetoresistance in small fields and negative magnetoresistance in strong fields (deGennes-Friedel formalism), hole-dominated transport, and anomalous Hall contribution at 2 K with multiband effects at higher temperatures
AI.conclusion: The compound shows complex magnetotransport behavior including possible field-induced Fermi surface reconstruction, with discrepancies between calculated bulk insulating behavior and observed semimetallic transport

title: Correlations Between the Dielectric Properties, Domain Structure Morphology and Phase State of Bi1-xSmxFeO3 Nanoparticles
summary: Nanoscale multiferroics are basic model objects for studying polar, magnetic and magnetoelectric properties and mutual couplings. Bismuth-samarium ferrite (Bi1-xSmxFeO3) is a model orthoferrite, whose polar, magnetic and magnetoelectric properties have been studied for the bulk and thin film samples. The properties of Bi1-xSmxFeO3 nanoparticles have been much less studied, despite the nanoparticles can be used in a wide range of applications, such as energy storage, magnetic hyperthermia and advanced nanoelectronics. In this work we performed experimental measurements and analysis of the temperature dependence of the Bi1-xSmxFeO3 nanopowders dielectric properties. Calculations of the ferro-ionic coupling influence on the dielectric properties, domain structure morphology and phase states are performed in the framework of the Ginzburg-Landau-Devonshire-Stephenson-Highland approach. Theoretical results explain the main trends of experimentally observed temperature dependences of the effective dielectric permittivity, which allows us to understand the correlations between the temperature behavior of dielectric properties, domain structure morphology and phase state of Bi1-xSmxFeO3 nanoparticles.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the dielectric properties of Bi1-xSmxFeO3 nanoparticles, combining experimental measurements of temperature-dependent dielectric behavior with theoretical calculations using the Ginzburg-Landau-Devonshire-Stephenson-Highland approach to understand the correlations between dielectric properties, domain structure morphology, and phase states.
AI.motivation: Nanoscale multiferroics are important model systems for studying polar, magnetic, and magnetoelectric properties. While bulk and thin film Bi1-xSmxFeO3 have been extensively studied, nanoparticles remain less explored despite their potential applications in energy storage, magnetic hyperthermia, and advanced nanoelectronics.
AI.method: Experimental measurements of temperature-dependent dielectric properties of Bi1-xSmxFeO3 nanopowders, combined with theoretical calculations using the Ginzburg-Landau-Devonshire-Stephenson-Highland approach to analyze ferro-ionic coupling effects on dielectric properties, domain structure morphology, and phase states.
AI.result: The theoretical calculations successfully explain the main trends of experimentally observed temperature dependences of effective dielectric permittivity, revealing correlations between temperature behavior of dielectric properties, domain structure morphology, and phase state of Bi1-xSmxFeO3 nanoparticles.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes a fundamental understanding of how temperature affects dielectric properties in Bi1-xSmxFeO3 nanoparticles through the relationship with domain structure and phase states, enabling better design of nanoparticle-based devices for various applications.

title: Magnetic landscape of NbTiN superconducting resonators under radio-frequency excitation
summary: Planar superconducting resonators are essential components in quantum circuits and highly sensitive sensors. However, their performance is often compromised by magnetic flux penetration, as the interaction of flux quanta and the induced radio-frequency (RF) currents in the superconducting thin film leads to significant energy dissipation. At low operating temperatures, this issue is aggravated as thermomagnetic instabilities can trigger the sudden propagation of magnetic flux avalanches. An important open question is whether the RF excitation itself stimulates the nucleation and propagation of magnetic flux avalanches in the superconducting thin film. The literature remains inconclusive on this point, partly due to the lack of compelling evidence for this phenomenon. In this work, we address this issue by unprecedented direct visualization of magnetic flux penetration through Faraday rotation imaging under simultaneous RF excitation. We demonstrate that the avalanche activity exhibits a weak dependence on the RF intensity for RF excitations within the linear Campbell regime. However, magnetic flux bursts clearly influence the RF transmission properties of the device. Furthermore, it is possible to unambiguously associate a particular avalanche event with a jump in resonance frequency. This enables us to identify the loci of most deleterious events and understand the distinct origins of upward and downward frequency shifts. These observations are supported by electromagnetic simulations in which local changes of the kinetic inductance mimic flux avalanches and confirm the invasive character of the MOI technique. The insights gained from this study aim to contribute to the broader understanding of the magnetic resilience of superconducting resonators, with the goal of improving their efficiency and stability.

AI.tldr: This study uses Faraday rotation imaging to directly visualize magnetic flux penetration in superconducting resonators under RF excitation, finding that avalanche activity has weak dependence on RF intensity in the linear regime, but flux bursts significantly affect resonance frequency, enabling identification of harmful event locations.
AI.motivation: Superconducting resonator performance is compromised by magnetic flux penetration causing energy dissipation, with thermomagnetic instabilities triggering flux avalanches. A key open question was whether RF excitation itself stimulates nucleation and propagation of magnetic flux avalanches, as the literature was inconclusive.
AI.method: Direct visualization of magnetic flux penetration through Faraday rotation imaging (MOI) under simultaneous RF excitation, combined with electromagnetic simulations where local changes of kinetic inductance mimic flux avalanches.
AI.result: Avalanche activity exhibits weak dependence on RF intensity within the linear Campbell regime. Magnetic flux bursts clearly influence RF transmission properties, and particular avalanche events can be unambiguously associated with jumps in resonance frequency. This enables identification of loci of most deleterious events and understanding of distinct origins of upward and downward frequency shifts.
AI.conclusion: The MOI technique is confirmed to be invasive. These insights contribute to understanding magnetic resilience of superconducting resonators, with potential for improving their efficiency and stability for quantum circuits and sensor applications.

title: Terahertz-nanoscale visualization of the microscopic spin-charge architecture of colossal magnetoresistive switching
summary: Resolving sub-10 nm spin switching and the associated terahertz (THz) electrodynamics during the colossal magnetoresistance (CMR) transition is a definitive frontier in reaching the fundamental spatial, temporal, and energy-dissipation limits of spin-based microelectronics and quantum logic architectures. Yet, the requirement of simultaneous control of high magnetic field, cryogenic environment, and nanometer-scale resolution has remained an elusive benchmark for terahertz nanoscopy, leaving the obscured nano-scale high-frequency dynamics of these transitions largely unexplored. Here, we overcome these limitations by utilizing a custom-built cryogenic magneto-THz scattering-type scanning near-field optical microscopy (cm-THz-sSNOM) platform to resolve the nanoscale, THz spectroscopic evolution of the magnetic field-driven CMR transition in a manganite single crystal $\text{Pr}_{2/3}\text{Ca}_{1/3}\text{MnO}_{3}$. Our measurements provide a real-space visualization of the local THz conductivity, capturing the moment that magnetic-field-induced spin switching triggers the phase transition from an antiferromagnetic insulator to a ferromagnetic metal. THz nano-imaging, together with an ellipsoidal near-field model, reveals a multi-scale transition initiated by 1-2 nm isolated spin-flip sites at low magnetic fields, which coalesce into $\sim$15~nm conducting regions as the threshold field is approached. These results provide an in situ, previously inaccessible THz real-space view of CMR switching, establishing a general analysis framework for mapping spin-charge-lattice-orbit-coupled dynamics at spatial scales that transcend the nominal sSNOM resolution.

AI.tldr: This paper presents cryogenic magneto-THz sSNOM to image the nanoscale magnetic field-driven colossal magnetoresistance transition in Pr2/3Ca1/3MnO3, revealing multi-scale spin switching from 1-2 nm isolated sites to ~15 nm conducting regions.
AI.motivation: Resolve the nanoscale THz dynamics during CMR transitions, which has been inaccessible due to the challenge of combining high magnetic fields, cryogenic temperatures, and nanometer-scale resolution in terahertz nanoscopy.
AI.method: Built a custom cryogenic magneto-THz scattering-type scanning near-field optical microscopy (cm-THz-sSNOM) platform to perform THz nano-imaging with an ellipsoidal near-field model on Pr2/3Ca1/3MnO3 manganite single crystals.
AI.result: Captured real-space visualization of local THz conductivity during magnetic-field-induced phase transition from antiferromagnetic insulator to ferromagnetic metal. Revealed multi-scale transition: 1-2 nm isolated spin-flip sites at low fields coalesce into ~15 nm conducting regions near threshold field.
AI.conclusion: Established an in situ THz real-space view of CMR switching and a general framework for mapping spin-charge-lattice-orbit-coupled dynamics at spatial scales beyond nominal sSNOM resolution, relevant for spin-based microelectronics and quantum logic architectures.

title: Universal electronic manifolds for extrapolative alloy discovery
summary: This study presents a computationally efficient framework for accelerated alloy discovery that uses the non-interacting electron density to capture intrinsic structure-property relationships in refractory high-entropy alloys (HEAs). Unlike state-of-the-art approaches relying on expensive, self-consistent density functional theory calculations, our method employs the non-interacting electron density as the primary structural descriptor. By extracting physical features through directionally resolved two-point spatial correlations and compressing them via Principal Component Analysis, we efficiently map the design space. Coupling these descriptors with Bayesian active learning, we achieve a normalized mean absolute error (NMAE) of <2% for the bulk modulus of Al-Nb-Ti-Zr alloys using only 10 training samples. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the model learns an electronic packing manifold that is transferable across distinct chemical species within refractory HEAs. Validated on a distinct 7-component refractory system (Mo-Nb-Ta-Ti-V-W-Zr) containing four elements entirely absent from the training data, the framework enables rigorous zero-shot extrapolation. Moreover, by augmenting the base model with just 20 samples from the target domain, we achieve high-fidelity predictions (NMAE < 3%) for 7-component alloys, reducing data acquisition costs by orders of magnitude compared to standard workflows. These results establish the non-interacting electron density as a rigorous, extrapolative descriptor for vast compositional landscapes.

AI.tldr: A computationally efficient framework using non-interacting electron density as a structural descriptor for accelerated discovery of refractory high-entropy alloys (HEAs), combined with Bayesian active learning to achieve <2% NMAE for bulk modulus predictions with only 10 training samples.
AI.motivation: Current approaches for alloy discovery rely on expensive self-consistent density functional theory (DFT) calculations, making exploration of vast compositional landscapes computationally prohibitive. There is a need for efficient descriptors that can capture intrinsic structure-property relationships without costly quantum mechanical calculations.
AI.method: The method uses non-interacting electron density as the primary structural descriptor, extracting physical features through directionally resolved two-point spatial correlations and compressing them via Principal Component Analysis (PCA). These descriptors are coupled with Bayesian active learning for efficient sampling.
AI.result: The framework achieves normalized mean absolute error (NMAE) of <2% for bulk modulus of Al-Nb-Ti-Zr alloys using only 10 training samples. It demonstrates transferability across distinct chemical species in refractory HEAs, including zero-shot extrapolation to a 7-component system (Mo-Nb-Ta-Ti-V-W-Zr) with four elements absent from training data. With just 20 additional samples from the target domain, NMAE < 3% is achieved for 7-component alloys.
