AI.conclusion: The non-interacting electron density serves as a rigorous, extrapolative descriptor for vast compositional landscapes, reducing data acquisition costs by orders of magnitude compared to standard DFT-based workflows while maintaining high prediction accuracy.

title: Discovery of intertwined pair density and charge density wave orders in UTe2
comment: Main text: 17 pages, 4 figures; Supplementary Information: 13 pages, 12 figures
summary: The strongly correlated spin-triplet superconductor UTe2 hosts an unusual landscape of magnetic-field-sensitive charge density wave (CDW) phases, positioning it as a compelling system for studying intertwined electronic orders. A central challenge is determining whether the observed charge modulations arise from a triplet pair density wave (PDW) order and, if so, how the anisotropic magnetic field response of triplet superconductivity is manifested in the CDW response. Here, using a scanning tunneling microscope equipped with a vector magnetic field, we systematically investigate the evolution and interrelation of distinct CDW orders. Complementing the previously identified incommensurate CDW peaks (qi=1,2,3), we resolve an additional set of nondispersive modulations (pi=1,2,3 and h1,2) with distinct temperature and magnetic field dependencies. The pi CDW peaks vanish near Tc, while the qi peaks survive well above Tc but are progressively suppressed by magnetic field in an anisotropic manner. The critical fields of the qi peaks mirror the directional hierarchy of Hc2, which suggests a PDW is present above the bulk Tc. This is consistent with a Landau free-energy picture where PDWs with wavevectors pi form above the bulk Tc, leading to composite CDW orders with wavevector qi. Below Tc, the coupling of PDWs and uniform superconductivity leads to the pi CDWs. Together, these findings establish UTe2 as a rare platform where both the parent PDW and descendant orders are directly resolved, enabling access to both the fundamental and emergent manifestations of PDW physics.

AI.tldr: Study investigates charge density wave (CDW) orders in spin-triplet superconductor UTe2 using scanning tunneling microscopy with vector magnetic field, discovering additional CDW modulations and establishing evidence for triplet pair density wave (PDW) orders above the bulk superconducting transition temperature.
AI.motivation: Determine whether observed charge modulations in UTe2 arise from triplet pair density wave (PDW) order and understand how anisotropic magnetic field response of triplet superconductivity manifests in CDW response.
AI.method: Scanning tunneling microscope (STM) equipped with vector magnetic field to systematically investigate evolution and interrelation of distinct CDW orders, complemented by temperature-dependent and magnetic field-dependent measurements.
AI.result: Resolved additional nondispersive modulations (pi=1,2,3 and h1,2) with distinct temperature and magnetic field dependencies. The pi CDW peaks vanish near Tc while qi peaks survive above Tc but are suppressed anisotropically by magnetic field. Critical fields of qi peaks mirror the directional hierarchy of Hc2.
AI.conclusion: Establishes UTe2 as a platform where both parent PDW and descendant orders are directly resolved. The Landau free-energy picture where PDWs with pi wavevectors form above bulk Tc, leading to composite CDW orders with qi wavevectors below Tc, is consistent with observations. This enables access to fundamental and emergent manifestations of PDW physics.

title: Magnetic phase diagram and spin Hamiltonian of antiferromagnet Cs$_2$CoI$_4$
summary: We report comprehensive thermodynamic and neutron scattering measurements on the $S$ = 3/2 antiferromagnet Cs$_2$CoI$_4$, a member of the thoroughly studied family of frustrated magnets Cs$_2MX_4$ ($M$ = Cu, Co, Ru, $X$ = Br, Cl, I, O). Unlike previously studied members, Cs$_2$CoI$_4$ undergoes a structural phase transition, for which we determine the low-temperature crystallographic structure. The resulting symmetry reduction strongly affects both the magnetic exchange interactions and single-ion anisotropy. Despite the large parameter space, we propose a minimal magnetic Hamiltonian that reasonably captures the observed excitation spectrum, analyzed using extended SU(4) linear spin-wave theory.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the S = 3/2 antiferromagnet Cs2CoI4, which undergoes a structural phase transition unlike other members of the Cs2MX4 family. Using neutron scattering and thermodynamic measurements combined with SU(4) linear spin-wave theory, the authors determine the low-temperature crystal structure and develop a minimal magnetic Hamiltonian that captures the excitation spectrum.
AI.motivation: Cs2CoI4 is a member of the well-studied Cs2MX4 family of frustrated magnets, but unlike previously studied members, it exhibits a structural phase transition that affects magnetic properties, making it an interesting candidate for understanding how structural changes influence magnetic behavior.
AI.method: Extended SU(4) linear spin-wave theory was applied to analyze the excitation spectrum, combined with comprehensive thermodynamic and neutron scattering measurements to characterize the material.
AI.result: The structural phase transition in Cs2CoI4 was characterized, revealing a low-temperature crystallographic structure that strongly modifies both magnetic exchange interactions and single-ion anisotropy. A minimal magnetic Hamiltonian was developed that reasonably captures the observed excitation spectrum.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully identifies a minimal magnetic Hamiltonian that captures the excitation spectrum of Cs2CoI4, demonstrating that the structural phase transition fundamentally alters the magnetic properties through changes in exchange interactions and single-ion anisotropy.

title: Exotic Cooperative Quantum Optics of Moire Exciton Superlattices
summary: The unique properties of two-dimensional moire systems have been widely studied from many perspectives. However, relatively little work has explored how the real space structure of the moire systems can directly engender novel properties and functionalities. In this work, we exploit the feature that moire excitons naturally form an ordered superlattice with a lattice constant comparable to the wavelength of the resonant light, which enables intriguing cooperative optical responses. Particularly, we show that the collective moire exciton states can have either strongly enhanced (superradiant) or suppressed (subradiant) radiative decay rate, depending on their in-plane wavevector. These super- and subradiant states can be efficiently switched by a gate-induced electric field gradient. Moreover, the cooperative transmittance $T$ of the nanometer-thick moire system can be switched from $T \approx 0$ (opaque) to $T \approx 1$ (transparent) with less than $2~\%$ heterostrain or a $1^{\circ}$ adjustment in the twist angle $θ$. These features are robust against non-radiative losses and inhomogeneity, making the moire system a highly versatile platform for cooperative quantum optics with potential applications in e.g., single photon storage and switching.

AI.tldr: This paper explores how the real space structure of 2D moire systems can create novel optical properties through naturally formed exciton superlattices. The research demonstrates that collective moire exciton states exhibit tunable radiative decay rates (superradiant or subradiant) dependent on in-plane wavevector, and that cooperative transmittance can be dramatically switched via small strain or twist angle adjustments.
AI.motivation: While the unique properties of 2D moire systems have been widely studied, relatively little work has explored how the real space structure of moire systems can directly engender novel properties and functionalities. The motivation is to exploit the feature that moire excitons naturally form an ordered superlattice with lattice constant comparable to resonant light wavelength, enabling intriguing cooperative optical responses.
AI.method: The study investigates collective moire exciton states and their radiative decay characteristics, examining how the in-plane wavevector affects superradiant (enhanced) and subradiant (suppressed) states. The researchers also explored gate-induced electric field gradients and strain/twist angle modifications to control the optical properties.
AI.result: The collective moire exciton states exhibit strongly enhanced (superradiant) or suppressed (subradiant) radiative decay rates depending on their in-plane wavevector. These states can be efficiently switched using gate-induced electric field gradients. Additionally, the cooperative transmittance of the nanometer-thick moire system can be switched from T ≈ 0 (opaque) to T ≈ 1 (transparent) with less than 2% heterostrain or 1° adjustment in twist angle θ.
AI.conclusion: These features are robust against non-radiative losses and inhomogeneity, making the moire system a highly versatile platform for cooperative quantum optics with potential applications in single photon storage and switching. The work demonstrates a new way to harness moire systems for quantum optical devices.

title: Microscopic theory of flexo Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya-type interaction
summary: We study interaction between two magnetic impurities mediated by itinerant electrons on the surface of curved magnets based on perturbation theory. We show that Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya type interaction can arise from inhomogeneous spin texture by bending, without any spin-orbit coupling. Analytical expressions of the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya type interaction are obtained. We demonstrate this effect in a one-dimensional ring model.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the interaction between two magnetic impurities mediated by itinerant electrons on curved magnet surfaces using perturbation theory. The authors show that Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya type interaction can arise from inhomogeneous spin texture by bending, without requiring spin-orbit coupling. Analytical expressions are derived and demonstrated in a one-dimensional ring model.
AI.motivation: To study the interaction between two magnetic impurities mediated by itinerant electrons on the surface of curved magnets, and to investigate whether Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya type interaction can arise from inhomogeneous spin texture by bending without spin-orbit coupling.
AI.method: Perturbation theory was used to study the interaction between two magnetic impurities mediated by itinerant electrons on the surface of curved magnets. The effect was demonstrated in a one-dimensional ring model.
AI.result: Analytical expressions of the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya type interaction were obtained, demonstrating that this interaction can arise from inhomogeneous spin texture by bending without any spin-orbit coupling.
AI.conclusion: Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya type interaction can arise from inhomogeneous spin texture by bending, without any spin-orbit coupling. This effect is demonstrated in a one-dimensional ring model.

title: A general statistical framework for vacancy and self-interstitial properties in concentrated multicomponent solids
summary: A rigorous understanding of the thermodynamic properties of point defects, namely vacancies and self-interstitials, is crucial for the discovery and screening of structural materials in clean energy applications. In this work, we extend a previously-developed statistical framework for predicting the thermodynamics of single-site impurities to further predict the thermodynamics of self-interstitial dumbbells in an arbitrarily complex alloy. We then apply this extended framework to compute effective formation energies in fully disordered Fe-Cr and Cu-Ni alloys. Notably, we predict that some self-interstitial dumbbell types that are high-energy in pure Fe become stabilized by Cr. We additionally describe a symmetry-breaking effect, wherein high solute concentrations distort the defect free energy surface, yielding misaligned self-interstitials.

AI.tldr: This paper extends a statistical framework to predict self-interstitial dumbbell thermodynamics in complex alloys. Applied to Fe-Cr and Cu-Ni alloys, the method reveals that Cr can stabilize otherwise high-energy self-interstitial configurations in Fe, and high solute concentrations cause symmetry-breaking that misaligns self-interstitials.
AI.motivation: A rigorous understanding of thermodynamic properties of point defects (vacancies and self-interstitials) is crucial for discovery and screening of structural materials in clean energy applications. This work aims to extend existing frameworks to predict thermodynamics of self-interstitial dumbbells in complex alloys.
AI.method: The researchers extended a previously-developed statistical framework for predicting thermodynamics of single-site impurities to predict thermodynamics of self-interstitial dumbbells in arbitrarily complex alloys. They then applied this extended framework to compute effective formation energies in fully disordered Fe-Cr and Cu-Ni alloys.
AI.result: The study predicts that some self-interstitial dumbbell types that are high-energy in pure Fe become stabilized by Cr. Additionally, a symmetry-breaking effect was discovered wherein high solute concentrations distort the defect free energy surface, yielding misaligned self-interstitials.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates the capability to predict thermodynamics of self-interstitial defects in complex alloys, revealing that Cr can stabilize high-energy self-interstitial configurations in Fe and that high solute concentrations induce symmetry-breaking effects on defect structures.

title: Stochastic Loop Corrections to Belief Propagation for Tensor Network Contraction
summary: Tensor network contraction is a fundamental computational challenge underlying quantum many-body physics, statistical mechanics, and machine learning. Belief propagation (BP) provides an efficient approximate solution, but introduces systematic errors on graphs with loops. Here, we introduce a hybrid method that achieves exact results by stochastically sampling loop corrections to BP and showcase our method by applying it to the two-dimensional ferromagnetic Ising model. For any pairwise Markov random field with symmetric edge potentials, our approach exploits an exact factorization of the partition function into the BP contribution and a loop correction factor summing over all valid loop configurations, weighted by edge weights derived directly from the potentials. We sample this sum using Markov chain Monte Carlo with moves that preserve the loop constraint, combined with umbrella sampling to ensure efficient exploration across all correlation strengths. Our stochastic approach provides unbiased estimates with controllable statistical error in any parameter regime.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a hybrid method that combines Belief Propagation with stochastic loop corrections to achieve exact tensor network contraction. The method factorizes the partition function into BP and loop correction terms, sampled via MCMC with umbrella sampling, providing unbiased estimates with controllable error for any pairwise Markov random field with symmetric edge potentials.
AI.motivation: Tensor network contraction is computationally challenging and underlies many important problems in quantum many-body physics, statistical mechanics, and machine learning. While Belief Propagation (BP) provides an efficient approximate solution, it introduces systematic errors on graphs with loops (loopy graphs). The need for exact results while maintaining computational efficiency was the primary motivation.
AI.method:
The method introduces a hybrid approach that combines:
1. Belief propagation (BP) as the base approximation
2. Stochastic sampling of loop corrections using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)
3. Umbrella sampling for efficient exploration across all correlation strengths
4. Edge weights derived directly from the underlying potentials

The key innovation is an exact factorization of the partition function into the BP contribution and a loop correction factor that sums over all valid loop configurations.
AI.result: The method achieves exact results by stochastically sampling loop corrections to Belief Propagation. It provides unbiased estimates with controllable statistical error in any parameter regime. The approach was demonstrated on the 2D ferromagnetic Ising model, but is generalizable to any pairwise Markov random field with symmetric edge potentials.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a principled solution to a long-standing problem in approximate inference. By combining belief propagation with stochastic loop corrections, the method achieves exactness while maintaining computational tractability. The approach is general and applicable to any pairwise Markov random field with symmetric edge potentials, making it broadly useful for computational physics and machine learning applications.

title: Bulk OsO2 Single Crystals: Superior Catalysts for Water Oxidation
comment: 36 pages,18 figures, published online at Advanced Functional Materials
summary: Although rutile RuO2 has been a well-known and almost the best oxygen evolution reaction (OER) catalyst, the OER properties for the similar rutile oxide OsO2 with the same group element with Ru have been unknown, mainly due to long-standing synthesis difficulties. In this work, we report the successful synthesis of high-quality OsO2 single crystals, and the ground micrometer-size single crystals are chemically stable in alkaline solutions and exhibit robust OER performance. In sharp contrast, OsO2 nanopowder reacts quickly with KOH solutions and cannot work for OER. Compared with commercial RuO2 nanopowder, the OsO2 single crystals show comparable catalytic current densities, remarkably lower overpotentials at high current densities and better stability. These findings question the universal applicability of nanoscaling and highlight crystal integrity as a key descriptor for achieving stable and efficient OER electrocatalysis.

AI.tldr: This study synthesizes OsO2 single crystals and demonstrates their excellent OER performance comparable to RuO2, while revealing that OsO2 nanopowder is unstable in alkaline conditions. The findings emphasize crystal integrity over nanoparticle size as the critical factor for stable OER electrocatalysis.
AI.motivation: Despite rutile RuO2 being a well-known excellent OER catalyst, the OER properties of OsO2 (same group element) remained unknown due to synthesis difficulties. This work aims to fill this knowledge gap.
AI.method: Successfully synthesized high-quality OsO2 single crystals and compared their OER performance with OsO2 nanopowder and commercial RuO2 nanopowder in alkaline solutions.
AI.result: OsO2 single crystals exhibit robust OER performance with comparable catalytic current densities, remarkably lower overpotentials at high current densities, and better stability compared to commercial RuO2 nanopowder. In contrast, OsO2 nanopowder reacts quickly with KOH and cannot work for OER.
AI.conclusion: Crystal integrity (rather than nanoscaling) is a key descriptor for achieving stable and efficient OER electrocatalysis. This challenges the universal applicability of nanoscaling in OER catalyst design.

title: Collinear spin density wave state in distorted square-lattice GdNiSn$_4$
summary: We characterize the magnetic ground state of the newly synthesized lanthanide intermetallic GdNiSn$_4$ via resonant elastic x-ray scattering measurements. This compound forms distorted square nets of Gd that initially order magnetically below 23 K followed by a lower temperature transition at 16 K. Our scattering data identify the ground state order as a single-$q$ incommensurate, collinear order that slides towards a commensurate wave vector above the 16 K transition. Magnetic symmetry analysis combined with azimuthal dependence resolves the ground state magnetic structure as a moment-modulated spin density wave state with Gd moments oriented parallel to the in-plane a-axis. We discuss connections between the observed magnetic order and electronic properties in this square-net compound.

AI.tldr: The magnetic ground state of the newly synthesized lanthanide intermetallic GdNiSn4 was characterized using resonant elastic x-ray scattering. The compound exhibits magnetic ordering below 23 K with a second transition at 16 K, identified as a single-q incommensurate, collinear order that becomes more commensurate at higher temperatures. The ground state is a moment-modulated spin density wave with Gd moments oriented parallel to the in-plane a-axis.
AI.motivation: The study aimed to understand the magnetic ordering behavior and ground state of GdNiSn4, a newly synthesized lanthanide intermetallic compound with distorted square nets of Gd, and to explore connections between magnetic order and electronic properties in such square-net compounds.
AI.method: Resonant elastic x-ray scattering (REXS) measurements were performed, combined with magnetic symmetry analysis and azimuthal dependence analysis to resolve the magnetic structure.
AI.result: The research identified the ground state as a single-q incommensurate, collinear order that slides towards a commensurate wave vector above the 16 K transition. The magnetic structure was resolved as a moment-modulated spin density wave state with Gd moments oriented parallel to the in-plane a-axis.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully characterized the magnetic ground state of GdNiSn4, providing insights into the relationship between magnetic ordering and electronic properties in lanthanide intermetallic compounds with square-net structures.

title: Crystal electric field excitations and spin dynamics in a spin-orbit coupled distorted honeycomb magnet BiErGeO$_5$
summary: The magnetic properties and crystal electric field (CEF) scheme of BiErGeO$_5$ are investigated via magnetization, heat capacity, muon spin relaxation (muSR), and inelastic neutron scattering (INS) experiments on a polycrystalline sample. The Er$^{3+}$ ions form a quasi-two-dimensional distorted honeycomb network with a Kramers doublet ground state. Magnetic susceptibility and heat capacity reveal short-range antiferromagnetic correlations, manifested as a broad maximum around 1.4 K. Heat-capacity data further confirm the onset of a magnetic long-range order at $T_ N = 0.4$ K. The INS spectra exhibit eight CEF excitations and the CEF analysis yields the $g$-factor anisotropy with $g_{xy}/g_{z} = 1.38$ and exchange anisotropy with $J_{xy} = 2.96$ K and $J_{z} = 1.56$ K. The experimental temperature and field dependent magnetization and heat capacity are also reproduced by the simulation using CEF energy scheme. Zero-field muSR measurements down to 30 mK, do not exhibit coherent oscillations or a static 1/3 tail. The spectra are well described by two exponential relaxation components, indicating two magnetically inequivalent muon environments. The relaxation rates display a nearly temperature-independent plateau below $T_{\rm N}$ and follow an Orbach-type activated behavior at higher temperatures involving excited CEF levels, consistent with the INS results. Longitudinal-field $μ$SR measurements reveal only weak decoupling up to 1.5 T, indicating persistent slow spin fluctuations below $T_{\rm N}$.

AI.tldr: Study investigates magnetic properties and crystal electric field scheme of BiErGeO5 using magnetization, heat capacity, μSR, and INS experiments. Er3+ ions form quasi-2D distorted honeycomb with Kramers doublet ground state, showing antiferromagnetic correlations and magnetic ordering at 0.4 K. μSR reveals persistent slow spin fluctuations without static long-range order.
AI.motivation: Investigate magnetic properties and crystal electric field (CEF) scheme of BiErGeO5, particularly the low-temperature magnetic behavior and spin dynamics in a quasi-2D distorted honeycomb Er3+ network
AI.method: Magnetization, heat capacity, muon spin relaxation (muSR), and inelastic neutron scattering (INS) experiments on polycrystalline sample, combined with CEF simulation
AI.result: Short-range antiferromagnetic correlations with broad maximum at 1.4 K; long-range magnetic order at TN = 0.4 K; eight CEF excitations observed; g-factor anisotropy gxy/gz = 1.38; exchange anisotropy Jxy = 2.96 K, Jz = 1.56 K; μSR shows two exponential relaxation components with temperature-independent plateau below TN and Orbach-type activated behavior at higher temperatures; weak decoupling up to 1.5 T indicates persistent slow spin fluctuations
AI.conclusion: The study successfully characterizes the magnetic properties of BiErGeO5, revealing a quasi-2D distorted honeycomb Er3+ network with Kramers doublet ground state. Zero-field μSR measurements indicate persistent slow spin fluctuations below TN without static long-range magnetic order, contrasting with the weak antiferromagnetic correlations observed. The CEF energy scheme successfully reproduces experimental magnetization and heat capacity data.

title: Revisiting the $J_1$-$J_2$ Heisenberg Model on a Triangular Lattice: Quasi-Degenerate Ground States and Phase Competition
summary: It is generally believed that the spin-$\tfrac{1}{2}$ triangular-lattice $J_1$-$J_2$ Heisenberg model hosts a quantum spin liquid in the intermediate regime between the $120^\circ$ and stripe ordered phases. Density matrix renormalization group studies on cylinders have consistently found two nearly degenerate ground states, commonly interpreted as distinct topological sectors. Using state-of-the-art matrix product state simulations on YC6 cylinders, we compare the static and dynamical properties of these two sectors at $J_2/J_1 = 0.125$. Noticeable differences appear already in static correlations; moreover, high-resolution dynamical structure factors reveal qualitatively distinct low-energy excitations. These results suggest that the two ground states cannot be understood as merely topologically distinct sectors of a gapped $\mathbb{Z}_2$ spin liquid.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the spin-1/2 triangular-lattice J1-J2 Heisenberg model using advanced matrix product state simulations, challenging the prevailing interpretation of two nearly degenerate ground states as topological sectors of a gapped Z2 spin liquid.
AI.motivation: To resolve the nature of two nearly degenerate ground states in the J1-J2 Heisenberg model that were previously interpreted as distinct topological sectors of a quantum spin liquid, using high-resolution numerical simulations.
AI.method: State-of-the-art matrix product state (MPS) simulations on YC6 cylinders at J2/J1 = 0.125, examining both static correlations and dynamical structure factors with high energy resolution.
AI.result: The two ground states exhibit noticeable differences in static correlations, and high-resolution dynamical structure factors reveal qualitatively distinct low-energy excitations between the two sectors.
AI.conclusion: The two ground states cannot be understood as merely topologically distinct sectors of a gapped Z2 spin liquid; they appear to represent fundamentally different physical states rather than topological sectors of the same phase.

title: Impacts of Fermi Level Pinning at Hole-Selective Contacts in CdSeTe/CdTe Solar Cells
summary: P-type doped CdTe free surfaces Schottky contacts, and even interfaces with isostructural p-ZnTe frequently exhibit downward band bending and moderate to high recombination velocities. Fermi level pinning by donor-like states can explain these band diagram features, as well as device response characteristics such as 1st quadrant rollover in current-voltage (JV) versus temperature (JVT). Parasitic downward band bending also produces voltage-dependent photocurrent collection, producing fill factor (FF) efficiency losses, JV dark/light non-superposition (or JV take-off), and irregularities in Jsc-Voc and Suns-Voc measurements. Herein, we develop a device physics model of state-of-the-art CdSeTe/CdTe solar cells consistent with known characterization of materials and devices, including the optical, thermalization, and trapping effects of band tail states and isolated defects. We use this model to demonstrate that Fermi-level pinning at the p-ZnTe/p-CdSeTe hole contact by donor-like defects reproduces the aforementioned observables, and conclude that (for contemporary few-um absorber thicknesses and low mobilities) it primarily affects FF rather than Voc. We investigate the performance gains possible from hypothetical passivated, hole-selective layers at the ZnTe/CdTe interface, which eliminate the downwards band bending caused by donor-like defects. For thinner devices and larger minority carrier diffusion lengths, these strategies will become more important for continued efficiency improvements.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a device physics model for CdSeTe/CdTe solar cells to explain performance issues caused by Fermi-level pinning at the p-ZnTe/p-CdSeTe hole contact. The model shows that donor-like defects cause downward band bending, leading to fill factor losses but minimal open-circuit voltage impacts for contemporary absorber thicknesses.
AI.motivation: P-type doped CdTe contacts exhibit downward band bending and high recombination velocities due to Fermi-level pinning by donor-like states, causing efficiency losses including fill factor degradation, JV dark/light non-superposition, and voltage-dependent photocurrent collection.
AI.method: Developed a comprehensive device physics model incorporating optical, thermalization, and trapping effects of band tail states and isolated defects, consistent with known materials and device characterization of state-of-the-art CdSeTe/CdTe solar cells.
AI.result: Fermi-level pinning at the p-ZnTe/p-CdSeTe hole contact by donor-like defects successfully reproduces the observed device characteristics. For typical few-um absorber thicknesses and low mobilities, the primary impact is on fill factor rather than open-circuit voltage.
AI.conclusion: Passivated hole-selective layers eliminating downward band bending could improve performance. These strategies will become more critical for thinner devices with larger minority carrier diffusion lengths to achieve continued efficiency improvements.

title: Understanding thermal and quantum fluctuations in extended Kitaev-Yao-Lee spin-orbital model
summary: Building upon the spin-1/2 Kitaev model on a honeycomb lattice, the Yao-Lee spin-orbital model provides exactly solvable quantum spin liquids with potentially better stability against perturbations due to the additional degree of freedom. Recently, the microscopic mechanism underlying the Yao-Lee interaction in honeycomb materials has been uncovered, leading to an extended Kitaev-Yao-Lee spin-orbital model when the celebrated Kugel-Khomskii interaction is included. Numerical studies of this model have identified various disordered phases, including a broad region of the nematic phase that is reminiscent of a spin-orbital liquid. Here, we investigate the origin and stability of this nematic phase via thermal and quantum fluctuations using classical Monte Carlo simulations and a generalized spin wave theory appropriate for the spin-orbital model. We demonstrate that the additional spin-orbital degree of freedom gives rise to strong thermal and quantum fluctuations in spin-orbital models, providing insight into the emergence of disordered phases.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the nematic phase in the extended Kitaev-Yao-Lee spin-orbital model on a honeycomb lattice using classical Monte Carlo simulations and generalized spin wave theory. The study reveals that the additional spin-orbital degree of freedom generates strong thermal and quantum fluctuations, explaining the emergence and stability of disordered phases.
AI.motivation: The Yao-Lee spin-orbital model, built upon the Kitaev model, offers exactly solvable quantum spin liquids with enhanced stability against perturbations. Recent discoveries uncovered the microscopic mechanism for Yao-Lee interactions in honeycomb materials, leading to an extended Kitaev-Yao-Lee model when including Kugel-Khomskii interactions. Numerical studies identified various disordered phases, particularly a broad nematic phase resembling a spin-orbital liquid, whose origin and stability needed investigation.
AI.method: The researchers employed two complementary approaches: classical Monte Carlo simulations to study thermal fluctuations, and a generalized spin wave theory specifically adapted for spin-orbital models to examine quantum fluctuations.
AI.result: The study demonstrates that the additional spin-orbital degree of freedom introduces strong thermal and quantum fluctuations in spin-orbital models. These fluctuations provide insight into the mechanism behind the emergence of disordered phases, particularly the nematic phase.
AI.conclusion: The spin-orbital degree of freedom plays a crucial role in stabilizing disordered phases through enhanced thermal and quantum fluctuations. This finding explains why the nematic phase in the Kitaev-Yao-Lee model exhibits characteristics of a spin-orbital liquid and has better stability compared to pure spin models.

title: High Thermal Conductivity in Back-End-of-Line Compatible AlN Thin Films
summary: With thermal issues becoming a major challenge to the development of integrated circuits (ICs), high-thermal-conductivity (high-TC) materials are gaining interest from both the industry and academia, especially for high-density back-end-of-line (BEOL) structures. Aluminum nitride (AlN) is an insulating material with high TC, suitable for thermal management in electronic devices. Furthermore, polycrystalline AlN thin films can be deposited at BEOL-compatible low temperatures (lower than 400 C) while retaining relatively high TC, rendering AlN a promising candidate for BEOL heat dissipation. Still, AlN deposition should aim at achieving high TC on a variety of substrates used in IC processes. In this work, we obtained 600- and 1200-nm BEOL-compatible AlN thin film samples on Si, SiO, SiN, and Al2O3 substrates for structural and thermal characterizations. Specifically, time-domain thermoreflectance (TDTR) measurements revealed consistently high TC (higher than 45 W m-1 K-1) on different substrates. Moreover, finite element analysis (FEA) simulations of AlN capped on top of an indium-tin-oxide (ITO) transistor showed a reduction of up to 44% in peak device temperature. Our work provided experimental and calculational evidence for the practicality of leveraging AlN as a high-TC, BEOL-compatible heat spreader material.

AI.tldr: This study demonstrates aluminum nitride (AlN) as a promising BEOL-compatible heat spreader material, achieving high thermal conductivity (>45 W m-1 K-1) on various substrates and reducing peak device temperatures by up to 44% in simulations.
AI.motivation: Thermal issues are a major challenge in integrated circuit development, particularly for high-density BEOL structures. There is a need for high-thermal-conductivity materials that can be deposited at BEOL-compatible low temperatures.
AI.method: Deposition of 600- and 1200-nm AlN thin films on Si, SiO2, SiN, and Al2O3 substrates using BEOL-compatible processes (<400°C); TDTR measurements for thermal conductivity characterization; FEA simulations of AlN-capped ITO transistor devices.
AI.result: TDTR measurements showed consistently high thermal conductivity (>45 W m-1 K-1) across different substrates. FEA simulations demonstrated up to 44% reduction in peak device temperature when AlN is used as a heat spreader.
AI.conclusion: AlN is a promising BEOL-compatible heat spreader material for thermal management in ICs, with potential to reduce peak device temperatures by up to 44%.

title: Orbital-Selective Engineering of Strain-Tunable Chern Insulators in Momentum Space
summary: Unlike conventional approaches where topological order is statically fixed post-synthesis, we demonstrate that a single external knob-strain-can independently modulate topological order and functional responses in the Tc-adsorbed penta-hexa silicene (Tc_PH-Si) monolayer, with both properties governed by a single microscopic mechanism: momentum-space orbital-selective engineering of Tc-dxz_Si-px hybridization. Combining first-principles calculations and tight-binding models, we show that biaxial strain drives a complete topological pathway: C=1 (0) to C=0 (-2) to C = -1 (-3 to -4) to C = 0 metallic state (-6). This is exemplified by two pivotal states: a topologically critical point yet functionally optimal state at -2 strain (C=0) hosting a direct bandgap (0.17 eV) and d11 = 8.34 pm_V, and a topologically nontrivial but equally optimal state at -4 strain (C = -1) with d11 = 11.01 pm_V-three times that of MoS2. Berry curvature analysis reveals that functionality arises from local orbital hybridization strength, while topology originates from its global phase distribution. This establishes a new paradigm for materials design, transforming static functional materials into dynamically tunable quantum platforms.

AI.tldr: A single external knob (strain) can independently modulate topological order and functional responses in Tc-adsorbed penta-hexa silicene monolayer through momentum-space orbital-selective engineering of Tc-d_xz_Si-p_x hybridization, demonstrating a complete topological pathway and achieving record piezoelectric response three times that of MoS₂.
AI.motivation: Conventional approaches fix topological order statically post-synthesis, limiting tunability. This work aims to demonstrate that a single external parameter (strain) can independently modulate both topological order and functional responses in materials, addressing the need for dynamically tunable quantum platforms.
AI.method: The study combines first-principles calculations with tight-binding models to investigate the Tc-adsorbed penta-hexa silicene monolayer under biaxial strain. Berry curvature analysis was employed to distinguish between functionality (arising from local orbital hybridization strength) and topology (originating from global phase distribution).
AI.result: Biaxial strain drives a complete topological pathway: C=1 (0) → C=0 (-2) → C=-1 (-3 to -4) → C=0 metallic state (-6). Two pivotal states were identified: (1) a topologically critical but functionally optimal state at -2% strain (C=0) with direct bandgap of 0.17 eV and d₁₁ = 8.34 pm/V; (2) a topologically nontrivial and equally optimal state at -4% strain (C=-1) with d₁₁ = 11.01 pm/V (three times that of MoS₂). Both functionality and topology originate from the same microscopic mechanism: momentum-space orbital-selective engineering of Tc-d_xz_Si-p_x hybridization.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a new paradigm for materials design by demonstrating that a single external knob (strain) can simultaneously control both topological order and functional responses through momentum-space orbital-selective engineering. The findings transform static functional materials into dynamically tunable quantum platforms, enabling independent modulation of topology and functionality via a unified microscopic mechanism.

title: Spin Neural Network Potential for Magnetic Phase Transitions in Uranium Dioxide
summary: Uranium dioxide (UO2) is a prototypical nuclear fuel material, yet predicting its thermophysical properties across a wide temperature range remains challenging. One factor contributing to this difficulty is the complex magnetic ordering at low temperatures, where spin-orbit coupling produces strong coupling between spin and lattice degrees of freedom. Direct DFT simulations of magnetic phase transitions at finite temperatures are computationally prohibitive. Here, we develop a spin neural network potential (SpinNNP) that explicitly incorporates spin degrees of freedom together with spin-orbit coupling to describe the magnetic states of UO2.Reference datasets were generated using magnetic constrained DFT+U calculations with spin-orbit coupling, covering a wide range of non-collinear spin configurations. The SpinNNP accurately reproduces DFT energies, atomic forces, spin forces, and lattice constants. Machine learning molecular dynamics simulations with spin dynamics successfully capture the antiferromagnetic-paramagnetic transition. Although the predicted magnetic ground state differs from experiment due to known limitations of the underlying DFT description, the transition temperature obtained is of the correct order of magnitude compared with experiment. These results demonstrate that machine-learning potentials can enable large-scale spin-lattice simulations of actinide oxides and provide a practical route toward predictive modeling of complex magnetic materials.

AI.tldr: A spin neural network potential (SpinNNP) is developed to describe magnetic states of UO2 by incorporating spin degrees of freedom with spin-orbit coupling, enabling machine learning molecular dynamics simulations that capture the antiferromagnetic-paramagnetic transition.
AI.motivation: Predicting thermophysical properties of UO2 across wide temperature ranges is challenging due to complex magnetic ordering at low temperatures with strong spin-lattice coupling, and direct DFT simulations of magnetic phase transitions at finite temperatures are computationally prohibitive.
AI.method: SpinNNP was developed using magnetic constrained DFT+U calculations with spin-orbit coupling, generating reference datasets covering non-collinear spin configurations. The potential was trained to reproduce DFT energies, atomic forces, spin forces, and lattice constants, then used for machine learning molecular dynamics with spin dynamics.
AI.result: SpinNNP accurately reproduces DFT properties and successfully captures the antiferromagnetic-paramagnetic transition. While the predicted magnetic ground state differs from experiment due to DFT limitations, the transition temperature is of correct magnitude compared to experiment.
AI.conclusion: Machine-learning potentials can enable large-scale spin-lattice simulations of actinide oxides and provide a practical route toward predictive modeling of complex magnetic materials.

title: The giant anomalous Hall and Nernst effects in Kagome permanent magnets RCo5
summary: Kagome lattice materials have attracted considerable attention due to their intriguing topological properties and potential applications in next-generation quantum and spintronic technologies. In particular, rare-earth permanent magnets with Kagome structure provide an ideal platform that combines robust magnetism with nontrivial quantum phenomena. However, their anomalous transport properties, particularly thermoelectric responses, remain insufficiently explored. In this work, we perform systematic first-principles calculations on the anomalous Hall and anomalous Nernst effects in Kagome permanent magnets RCo5 (R = Ce, La, Sm, Gd). We find that CeCo5 exhibits a pronounced anomalous Hall conductivity of about 1500 Omega^-1 cm^-1 while GdCo5 displays a substantial anomalous Nernst conductivity of 11 A m^-1 K^-1 within +/- 0.1 eV of the Fermi energy, both comparable to or surpassing the measured intrinsic values reported in many typical Weyl and Heusler magnets. These exceptional anomalous transport properties originate from Berry curvature hotspots near spin-orbit coupling induced band gaps. If validated, these theoretical predictions would be important for Berry-curvature-driven transport in magnetic intermetallics. Our results establish RCo5 compounds as versatile platforms for exploring Berry curvature-driven transport in tunable magnetic topological materials.

AI.tldr: First-principles study predicts exceptional anomalous Hall and Nernst effects in Kagome magnets RCo5 (R=Ce, La, Sm, Gd), with CeCo5 showing ~1500 Ω^-1 cm^-1 Hall conductivity and GdCo5 showing ~11 A m^-1 K^-1 Nernst conductivity, originating from Berry curvature hotspots near SOC-induced band gaps.
AI.motivation: Kagome lattice materials have intriguing topological properties and potential applications in quantum/spintronic technologies, but their thermoelectric responses remain insufficiently explored despite rare-earth permanent magnets with Kagome structure offering ideal platforms combining robust magnetism with quantum phenomena.
AI.method: Systematic first-principles calculations on anomalous Hall and anomalous Nernst effects in Kagome permanent magnets RCo5 (R = Ce, La, Sm, Gd)
AI.result: CeCo5 exhibits pronounced anomalous Hall conductivity (~1500 Ω^-1 cm^-1) while GdCo5 displays substantial anomalous Nernst conductivity (~11 A m^-1 K^-1) within ±0.1 eV of Fermi energy, both comparable to or surpassing typical Weyl and Heusler magnets. These properties originate from Berry curvature hotspots near spin-orbit coupling induced band gaps.
AI.conclusion: RCo5 compounds (especially CeCo5 and GdCo5) are identified as versatile platforms for Berry curvature-driven transport in magnetic topological materials, with potential for next-generation quantum and spintronic applications.

title: A defect in diamond with millisecond-scale spin relaxation time at room temperature
summary: Spin defects in diamond are promising platforms for quantum sensing. The longest electron spin relaxation times ($T_1$) at room temperature for solid-state defects are observed in nitrogen vacancy centers in diamond, which can reach 6.67 ms, and substitutional nitrogen ("P1 centers") in diamond, which exhibit a $T_1$ of 2 ms. No other solid-state defect has exhibited millisecond-scale spin relaxation times at room temperature thus far. Here, we characterize the spin properties of the WAR5 defect in diamond with pulsed electron spin resonance. The observed $T_1$ is one of the longest for solid-state spin defects: 0.97(27) ms at room temperature and 14.38(19) min at 4 K. The observed coherence time ($T_2$) is 246(7) $μ$s, which can be extended to 6.49(34) ms at 4 K with dynamical decoupling. Furthermore, we demonstrate optical spin polarization with a range of wavelengths from 405 nm to 500 nm and propose potential zero-phonon line candidates.

AI.tldr: This paper characterizes the WAR5 defect in diamond, demonstrating exceptionally long spin relaxation times (T1 of 0.97 ms at room temperature and 14.38 minutes at 4 K), making it one of the longest-lived solid-state spin defects for quantum sensing applications.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to identify alternative spin defects in diamond with even longer spin relaxation times than the well-studied nitrogen vacancy (NV) centers and P1 centers, which currently hold the records for longest T1 times (6.67 ms and 2 ms respectively) at room temperature among solid-state defects.
AI.method: The researchers used pulsed electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy to characterize the spin properties of the WAR5 defect, including measurements of spin-lattice relaxation time (T1), spin-spin coherence time (T2), and dynamical decoupling techniques to extend coherence times. They also investigated optical spin polarization across wavelengths from 405 nm to 500 nm.
AI.result: The WAR5 defect exhibited a T1 of 0.97(27) ms at room temperature (ranking among the longest for solid-state spin defects) and remarkably 14.38(19) minutes at 4 K. The T2 coherence time was measured at 246(7) μs, which could be extended to 6.49(34) ms at 4 K using dynamical decoupling. Optical spin polarization was demonstrated across the 405-500 nm wavelength range, with potential zero-phonon line candidates identified.
AI.conclusion: The WAR5 defect in diamond shows exceptional spin properties that make it a highly promising candidate for quantum sensing applications, particularly for experiments requiring long spin relaxation times. The combination of millisecond-scale T1 at room temperature, minute-scale T1 at cryogenic temperatures, and extendable coherence times positions WAR5 as a competitive alternative to NV centers for quantum sensing technologies.

title: Heavy-Fermion Behavior and a Tunable Density Wave in a Novel Vanadium-based Mosaic Lattice
summary: The pursuit of geometrically frustrated lattices beyond conventional paradigms remains a central challenge in the design of quantum materials. Herein, we report the discovery of Cs3V9Te13 (CVT), a novel intermetallic compound that hosts a unique two-dimensional vanadium mosaic lattice, composed of an ordered tessellation of triangles, squares, and pentagons, bearing profound structural kinship with the celebrated kagome lattice. Remarkably, CVT exhibits behavior analogous to heavy fermion systems, characterized by a large Sommerfeld coefficient(γ = 425 mJ mol-1 K-2) and a coherent density-wave-like (DW-like) transition at T* = 47 K. This establishes CVT as a rare and intriguing example of a strongly correlated system. Inspired by pressure-tuning in related compounds, we demonstrate that this ground state is exquisitely tunable via chemical pressure. Systematic substitution of Cs with smaller Rb ions suppresses the DW-like order while strongly weakening the heavy-electron response, ultimately driving the system into a distinct non-magnetic, semiconducting, quantum-disordered state above 60 mK. This work unveils a new arena for exploring the interplay between heavy-fermion physics, density waves, and quantum disorder. The mosaic lattice in Cs3V9Te13 provides an unprecedented, chemically controllable platform for navigating the phase space between distinct correlated electronic states.

AI.tldr: Cs3V9Te13 (CVT) is a newly discovered intermetallic compound featuring a unique 2D vanadium mosaic lattice (triangles, squares, pentagons) related to the kagome lattice. It exhibits heavy fermion behavior with a large Sommerfeld coefficient (425 mJ mol-1 K-2) and a density-wave-like transition at 47 K. Substituting Rb for Cs suppresses this ground state and drives the system into a quantum-disordered state.
AI.motivation: The pursuit of geometrically frustrated lattices beyond conventional paradigms remains a central challenge in quantum materials design. The authors sought to discover new compounds that could provide insights into the interplay between heavy-fermion physics, density waves, and quantum disorder.
AI.method: Discovery of a novel intermetallic compound Cs3V9Te13 (CVT) through material synthesis. Investigation of its physical properties through measurements of Sommerfeld coefficient and transport properties. Chemical pressure tuning via systematic substitution of Cs with smaller Rb ions to study phase evolution.
AI.result: CVT exhibits heavy fermion behavior with γ = 425 mJ mol-1 K-2 and a coherent density-wave-like transition at T* = 47 K. Rb substitution suppresses the DW-like order, weakens the heavy-electron response, and drives the system into a non-magnetic, semiconducting, quantum-disordered state above 60 mK.
AI.conclusion: CVT represents a rare example of a strongly correlated system with a chemically controllable mosaic lattice. It provides an unprecedented platform for exploring the phase space between heavy-fermion physics, density waves, and quantum disorder through chemical tuning.

title: Impact of Layer Structure and Strain on Morphology and Electronic Properties of InAs Quantum Wells on InP (001)
summary: High-quality InAs quantum wells grown on InP are a promising platform for topological quantum information processing due to their large g-factor, strong Rashba spin-orbit interaction, and their compatibility with in-situ-deposited superconductors. In this work, we investigate InAs/InGaAs quantum wells grown on InP (001) wafers, focusing on how the layer structure and strain influence the electronic properties and surface morphology. By combining quantum transport measurements with atomic force microscopy, we show that the layer design predominantly affects the mobility anisotropy, which aligns well with the surface morphology. Surface characterization further reveals the mechanism of quantum well collapse when the layer thickness exceeds the strain limit. In addition, transport measurements demonstrate that quantum confinement has a clear impact on band nonparabolicity.

AI.tldr: InAs/InGaAs quantum wells on InP (001) substrates are promising for topological quantum computing due to large g-factors and strong spin-orbit coupling. This work investigates how layer structure and strain affect electronic properties and surface morphology.
AI.motivation: High-quality InAs quantum wells on InP are promising for topological quantum information processing due to their large g-factor, strong Rashba spin-orbit interaction, and compatibility with in-situ-deposited superconductors. However, the relationship between layer design, strain, electronic properties, and surface morphology needed investigation.
AI.method: The researchers combined quantum transport measurements with atomic force microscopy to investigate InAs/InGaAs quantum wells grown on InP (001) wafers, focusing on how layer structure and strain influence electronic properties and surface morphology.
AI.result: Layer design predominantly affects mobility anisotropy, which aligns well with surface morphology. Surface characterization revealed the mechanism of quantum well collapse when layer thickness exceeds the strain limit. Transport measurements demonstrated that quantum confinement has a clear impact on band nonparabolicity.
AI.conclusion: The study provides important insights for optimizing InAs/InGaAs quantum well structures for topological quantum computing applications by establishing the relationship between layer design, strain, electronic properties, and surface morphology.

title: Averaging Molecular Dynamics simulations to study the slow-strain rate behavior of metals
summary:
The application of molecular dynamics (MD) simulations to quasistatic loading is severely limited by the large separation between atomic vibration timescales and experimentally relevant deformation rates. In this work we employ the Practical Time Averaging (PTA) framework to overcome this limitation and enable atomistic simulations of crystalline solids under quasistatic loading conditions. PTA exploits the intrinsic separation of timescales by defining slow variables as time-averaged observables of the fast atomistic dynamics and their evolution on the slow loading timescale, thereby avoiding explicit integration of the fast dynamics.
  Using this approach, we simulate uniaxial deformation, in both tension and compression, of 4 to 20 nm cubic specimens of face centered cubic aluminum nanocrystals at applied strain rates approaching quasistatic conditions. We define slow variables as the averaged kinetic energy, potential energy, and normal stress in the loading direction, and track their evolution on the slow timescale. The stress-strain curves show yielding close to the theoretical stress for homogeneous nucleation, followed by successive load drops and rises caused by dislocation nucleation, motion, and exit from free surfaces. The "smaller is harder" effect is evident from the stress-strain response and from the variation of yield stress with sample size. Serrations in the response are more pronounced for smaller samples. The effects of applied strain rate and initial temperature are also studied. The method also captures the evolution of intricate dislocation microstructures on the slow timescale by tracking time-averaged atomic positions. The PTA framework enables simulations at strain rates several orders of magnitude lower than those accessible to conventional MD, demonstrating significant speedup in computational time while retaining full atomistic resolution.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the Practical Time Averaging (PTA) framework to overcome timescale limitations in molecular dynamics simulations of crystalline solids under quasistatic loading. By defining slow variables as time-averaged observables of fast atomistic dynamics, simulations of 4-20 nm aluminum nanocrystals at near-quasistatic strain rates become feasible, revealing dislocation behavior and size effects while achieving major computational speedup.
AI.motivation: Molecular dynamics simulations for quasistatic loading are severely limited by the large timescale gap between atomic vibrations (picoseconds) and experimentally relevant deformation rates (seconds to hours). Conventional MD cannot access quasistatic loading conditions.
AI.method: The Practical Time Averaging (PTA) framework defines slow variables as time-averaged observables (kinetic energy, potential energy, normal stress) of fast atomistic dynamics, tracking their evolution on the slow loading timescale without explicit integration of fast dynamics. Uniaxial tension and compression simulations were performed on 4-20 nm cubic FCC aluminum nanocrystals, with time-averaged atomic positions used to track dislocation microstructures.
AI.result: Stress-strain curves show yielding near theoretical homogeneous nucleation stress, followed by load drops and rises from dislocation activity. The "smaller is harder" effect is evident with more pronounced serrations in smaller samples. The method captures intricate dislocation microstructures evolution while achieving significant computational speedup.
AI.conclusion: The PTA framework successfully enables atomistic simulations at strain rates several orders of magnitude lower than conventional MD while maintaining full atomistic resolution. The method captures dislocation nucleation, motion, and exit from free surfaces, as well as the "smaller is harder" size effect in nanocrystals.

title: Microstructural origins of energy storage during plastic deformation of 310S TWIP steel
summary: The microstructural mechanisms governing energy storage during plastic deformation of twinning-induced plasticity (TWIP) steels remain insufficiently understood, particularly under conditions of strain localization. This study provides a crystallographic-scale interpretation of energy storage in 310S TWIP steel exhibiting complex deformation mechanisms. Electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) was used to characterize the evolution of local crystallographic orientation and microtexture during uniaxial tensile deformation using two complementary approaches: tracking the same surface region at successive strain levels and analysing regions corresponding to known local plastic strain. Deformation was initially dominated by dislocation slip, while twinning activity increased significantly beyond an equivalent plastic strain of approximately 0.3. Progressive deformation produced pronounced lattice rotations and the development of a dual-fibre texture consisting of a dominant 111 parallel to RD component and a secondary 100 parallel to RD component associated with deformation twinning. Correlation with previously quantified energy storage behaviour obtained from coupled digital image correlation and infrared thermography measurements reveals that intensified twinning and texture evolution in strain-localized regions are accompanied by a marked reduction in the energy storage rate. The results indicate that twin-matrix refinement and lattice rotation progressively reduce the material's capacity to store deformation energy and create favourable conditions for shear-band-mediated deformation.

AI.tldr: This study investigates the microstructural mechanisms governing energy storage in 310S TWIP steel during plastic deformation, particularly under strain localization conditions. Using EBSD to track crystallographic orientation evolution and correlating with digital image correlation and infrared thermography measurements, researchers found that deformation is initially dominated by dislocation slip, with twinning activity increasing significantly beyond ~0.3 equivalent plastic strain. A dual-fibre texture develops (111 || RD dominant, 100 || RD secondary). Importantly, intensified twinning and texture evolution in strain-localized regions are accompanied by a marked reduction in energy storage rate, indicating that twin-matrix refinement and lattice rotation progressively reduce the material's capacity to store deformation energy.
AI.motivation: The microstructural mechanisms governing energy storage during plastic deformation of TWIP steels remain insufficiently understood, particularly under conditions of strain localization. Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for improving the material's performance and formability.
AI.method: EBSD was used to characterize the evolution of local crystallographic orientation and microtexture during uniaxial tensile deformation using two complementary approaches: tracking the same surface region at successive strain levels and analysing regions corresponding to known local plastic strain. Results were correlated with coupled digital image correlation and infrared thermography measurements.
AI.result: Deformation was initially dominated by dislocation slip, while twinning activity increased significantly beyond an equivalent plastic strain of ~0.3. Progressive deformation produced pronounced lattice rotations and developed a dual-fibre texture (dominant 111 || RD component and secondary 100 || RD component associated with deformation twinning). Intensified twinning and texture evolution in strain-localized regions were accompanied by a marked reduction in the energy storage rate.
AI.conclusion: Twin-matrix refinement and lattice rotation progressively reduce the material's capacity to store deformation energy and create favorable conditions for shear-band-mediated deformation.

title: The role of austenite twins on variant selection during decomposition in low carbon steels
summary: Thermomechanical Controlled Processing (TMCP) is widely used to control the microstructure and properties of linepipe or high strength low alloy steels (HSLA). These steels are often joined by welding and used in demanding environments such as the Arctic. In these materials, the thermal path the steel experiences is critical for understanding microstructural evolution during processing. A key step is the solid-state phase transformation during cooling from the high-temperature austenite to the room-temperature microstructure which significantly influences the final mechanical properties. Specifically, the population of different variants and grain shapes that form affect the types and morphology of the grains, and grain boundary network which influence strength and toughness of the final component. In this paper, we apply 3D microscopy using a Xe-plasma focussed ion beam scanning electron microscope (pFIB-SEM) which is equipped with electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) in a static configuration to characterize the room temperature microstructure of a steel sample, and then use a '2.5D' prior austenite grain (PAG) reconstruction code to explore the relationship between the austenite phase and the room temperature microstructure. A significant result for the present work is the collection, and analysis, of data from a large volume (150 x 150 x 100 μm3, with a (200 nm)3 voxel size) which enables analysis of a complete prior austenite grain. Analysis of variants within this grain demonstrates that high-temperature twin boundaries likely govern variant selection and grain growth in the child microstructure. This suggests opportunities to engineer novel microstructures by controlling the high-temperature grain boundary character.

AI.tldr: This paper uses 3D pFIB-SEM/EBSD and 2.5D prior austenite grain reconstruction to analyze the relationship between austenite phase and room-temperature microstructure in TMCP steels. Analysis of a large volume (150×150×100 μm³) reveals that high-temperature twin boundaries control variant selection and grain growth, offering opportunities for microstructure engineering through high-temperature grain boundary character control.
AI.motivation: Understanding the solid-state phase transformation during cooling from high-temperature austenite to room-temperature microstructure is critical for controlling microstructural evolution and final mechanical properties in TMCP steels used in demanding environments like the Arctic.
AI.method: 3D microscopy using a Xe-plasma focused ion beam scanning electron microscope (pFIB-SEM) with electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) in a static configuration, combined with a '2.5D' prior austenite grain (PAG) reconstruction code.
AI.result: Analysis of a large volume (150 x 150 x 100 μm³ with 200 nm voxel size) containing a complete prior austenite grain demonstrated that high-temperature twin boundaries govern variant selection and grain growth in the child microstructure.
AI.conclusion: High-temperature twin boundaries likely govern variant selection and grain growth in the child microstructure, suggesting opportunities to engineer novel microstructures by controlling the high-temperature grain boundary character.

title: A Perspective on Training Machine Learning Force Fields for Solid-State Electrolyte Materials
summary: Machine learning force fields enable high-accuracy modeling of solid-state electrolytes (SSEs). This perspective evaluates dataset size, reference quality, and model architectures. We show that rigid SSE frameworks favor efficient learning, prioritizing data quality over quantity. Crucially, force RMSE does not reliably predict transport performance. By analyzing locality and benchmarking frameworks, we provide practical guidelines to accelerate the development of next-generation solid-state batteries.

AI.tldr: This perspective evaluates machine learning force fields for solid-state electrolytes, showing that rigid SSE frameworks prioritize data quality over quantity, and that force RMSE is not a reliable predictor of transport performance.
AI.motivation: To accelerate development of next-generation solid-state batteries by providing practical guidelines for machine learning force field development for solid-state electrolytes.
AI.method: Evaluated dataset size, reference quality, and model architectures; analyzed locality and benchmarked frameworks for solid-state electrolyte modeling.
AI.result: Rigid SSE frameworks favor efficient learning; data quality is more important than quantity; force RMSE does not reliably predict transport performance.
AI.conclusion: Practical guidelines are provided to accelerate development of next-generation solid-state batteries through optimized machine learning force field approaches.

title: Defect Detection in Magnetic Systems Using U-Net and Statistical Measures
summary: Local material inhomogeneities can strongly influence magnetization dynamics and macroscopic magnetic properties, yet detecting such defects from magnetic imaging data remains challenging when thermal fluctuations and experimental noise obscure static contrast. Here, we investigate defect detection in strongly fluctuating magnetization regimes where signatures of inhomogeneities largely average out in time-resolved measurements. Using finite-temperature micromagnetic simulations with randomly distributed defects and material parameters representative of \ce{Ni80Fe20}, we compute per-pixel temporal mean, temporal standard deviation, and latent entropy and use them as inputs for U-Net-based semantic segmentation models. We find that the most effective descriptor depends on the noise level and, importantly, that robust detection requires training data that reflect the expected noise statistics. These results provide practical guidance for designing noise-robust defect-detection workflows in magnetic imaging.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses detecting material defects in magnetic imaging under strong thermal fluctuations using finite-temperature micromagnetic simulations and U-Net segmentation, finding that optimal detection descriptors depend on noise level and training data must match expected noise statistics.
AI.motivation: Local material inhomogeneities strongly influence magnetization dynamics and macroscopic magnetic properties, but detecting such defects from magnetic imaging data is challenging when thermal fluctuations and experimental noise obscure static contrast
AI.method: Finite-temperature micromagnetic simulations with randomly distributed defects, using temporal mean, temporal standard deviation, and latent entropy as inputs for U-Net-based semantic segmentation models
AI.result: The most effective descriptor for defect detection depends on the noise level, and robust detection requires training data that reflects expected noise statistics
AI.conclusion: Robust defect detection in magnetic imaging requires training data that matches expected noise statistics, and the most effective descriptor varies with noise level.

title: AI-Driven Phase Identification from X-ray Hyperspectral Imaging of cycled Na-ion Cathode Materials
summary: Na-ion batteries have emerged as viable candidates for large-scale energy storage applica- tions due to resource abundance and cost advantages. The constraints imposed on their performance and durability, for instance, by complex phase transformations in positive electrode materials during electrochemical cycling, can be addressed and are thus not detrimental to their development. However, diffusion-limited Na-ion transport can drive spatially heterogeneous phase nucleation and propagation, leading to multiphase coexis- tence and locally non-uniform electrochemical activity, generating complex reaction path- ways that challenge both mechanistic understanding and predictive material optimization. These challenges can be addressed by investigating single-crystalline regions of materials, i.e. down to the scale of individual particles, although such analyses are often constrained by energetically and/or spatially sparse hyperspectral datasets. Here, we developed an AI-driven method to process hyperspectral data under sparse sampling conditions and generate multiphase maps with nanometer-scale resolution over a micrometer-scale field of view. We applied this processing on scanning transmission X-ray microscopy (STXM) data to determine the distribution and coexistence of phases in individual particles of NaxV2(PO4)2F3 cathode materials, at different states of charge. The methodology relies on a workflow which combines a Gaussian mixture variational autoencoder (GMVAE) algorithm with the Pearson corre- lation coefficient to identify the sodium content and map their spatial distribution. Our approach reveals nanoscale phase heterogeneity and evolution within individual particles, and improves the reliability of phase detection by identifying ambiguity zones, false assign- ments, and transition phases localized at grain boundaries.

AI.tldr: AI-driven method combining GMVAE and Pearson correlation processes sparse hyperspectral STXM data to map multiphase distribution in NaxV2(PO4)2F3 cathode materials at nanometer resolution, revealing nanoscale phase heterogeneity and identifying ambiguity zones at grain boundaries.
AI.motivation: Na-ion batteries face challenges from complex phase transformations and diffusion-limited Na-ion transport causing heterogeneous phase nucleation, multiphase coexistence, and non-uniform electrochemical activity in positive electrode materials, requiring investigation at individual particle scale.
AI.method: Developed AI-driven methodology processing hyperspectral data under sparse sampling conditions by combining Gaussian mixture variational autoencoder (GMVAE) algorithm with Pearson correlation coefficient, applied to scanning transmission X-ray microscopy (STXM) data.
AI.result: Generated multiphase maps with nanometer-scale resolution over micrometer-scale field of view for NaxV2(PO4)2F3 cathode materials at different states of charge, revealing nanoscale phase heterogeneity and evolution within individual particles, while identifying ambiguity zones, false assignments, and transition phases localized at grain boundaries.
AI.conclusion: The GMVAE-Pearson workflow improves reliability of phase detection in Na-ion battery cathode materials by accurately mapping phase distribution and coexistence at nanoscale, addressing challenges in mechanistic understanding and predictive material optimization.

title: Machine Learning for Electrode Materials: Property Prediction via Composition
summary: In this work, we benchmark three leading Machine Learning (ML) frameworks-MODNet, CrabNet, and a random forest model based on Magpie feature-for predicting properties of battery electrode materials using the Materials Project Battery Explorer dataset. We evaluate these models based on predictive accuracy, visualize numerical features using two-dimensional embeddings, and quantify performance using standard metrics. Our results demonstrate that CrabNet consistently outperforms the other models across all tests. To validate these findings, we employ robust statistical methods: bootstrap resampling and two cross-validation (CV) strategies (leave one cluster out and stratified 5-fold CV), comparing each model against a control baseline. In addition, we apply unsupervised clustering on MODNet-derived features using t-SNE and DBSCAN, revealing coherent material groupings without prior labels. This analysis confirms the robustness of the evaluated models and underscores the potential of ML-driven approaches for accelerating the electrode materials discovery. However, our study also identifies practical limitations and quantifies challenges associated with integrating ML models into materials science workflows. Despite these constraints, our findings suggest that ML models are highly effective for early-stage compositional screening in the battery industry. This work provides a foundation for future research on ML applications in materials discovery.

AI.tldr: This paper benchmarks three ML frameworks (MODNet, CrabNet, and random forest with Magpie features) for predicting battery electrode material properties using the Materials Project Battery Explorer dataset. CrabNet demonstrated superior performance across all evaluation metrics.
AI.motivation: The research aims to evaluate and compare leading ML frameworks for predicting properties of battery electrode materials, addressing the need for accelerated materials discovery in the battery industry through ML-driven approaches.
AI.method: The study employed bootstrap resampling and two cross-validation strategies (leave one cluster out and stratified 5-fold CV) for robust statistical validation. Two-dimensional embeddings were visualized, and unsupervised clustering using t-SNE and DBSCAN was applied to MODNet-derived features.
AI.result: CrabNet consistently outperformed both MODNet and the random forest model across all tests. The t-SNE and DBSCAN clustering revealed coherent material groupings without prior labels, confirming the robustness of the evaluated models.
AI.conclusion: ML models, particularly CrabNet, are highly effective for early-stage compositional screening in the battery industry. However, practical limitations and challenges remain for integrating ML models into materials science workflows.

title: Understanding halide segregation in metal halide perovskites through defect thermodynamics
comment: 25 pages, 6 figures + 13 pages of Supporting Information
summary: Halide segregation in metal halide perovskites limits their bandgap tunability and hinders their adoption in tandem solar cells and light emitting diodes. Here, we reveal the thermodynamic driving force behind halide segregation in mixed halide (Br-I) perovskites. By performing first-principles calculations on slab models with varying bromide and iodide distributions, we demonstrate that bromide ions preferentially occupy surface sites over bulk sites. Our simulations show that the segregation tendency is higher in MAPb(Br$_x$I$_{1-x}$)$_3$ (MA=methylammonium) compared to FA$_{0.8}$Cs$_{0.2}$Pb(Br$_x$I$_{1-x}$)$_3$, highlighting the role of the A-site cation. To quantify this effect, we establish a descriptor for halide segregation: the difference in defect formation energies of Br antisite defects between the bulk and the surface, which confirms the role of the A-site cation at equimolar Br-I concentration. Furthermore, we identify the localization of photo-generated holes near iodide ions, which triggers their oxidation and accelerates the formation of iodide vacancies, thereby promoting segregation. Overall, this work establishes defect thermodynamics as a framework for understanding halide segregation and provides a structural basis for designing stable mixed halide perovskites.

AI.tldr: This study reveals the thermodynamic driving force behind halide segregation in mixed halide perovskites using first-principles calculations, showing that bromide ions preferentially occupy surface sites. The A-site cation plays a role in segregation tendency, with MAPb(Br_xI_{1-x})_3 showing higher segregation than FA_{0.8}Cs_{0.2}Pb(Br_xI_{1-x})_3. Photo-generated hole localization near iodide ions promotes segregation by accelerating iodide vacancy formation.
AI.motivation: Halide segregation in metal halide perovskites limits their bandgap tunability and hinders their adoption in tandem solar cells and light emitting diodes. The paper aims to reveal the thermodynamic driving force behind halide segregation in mixed halide (Br-I) perovskites.
AI.method: First-principles calculations on slab models with varying bromide and iodide distributions were performed. The researchers established a descriptor for halide segregation: the difference in defect formation energies of Br antisite defects between the bulk and the surface.
AI.result: Bromide ions preferentially occupy surface sites over bulk sites. The segregation tendency is higher in MAPb(Br$_x$I$_{1-x}$)$_3$ compared to FA$_{0.8}$Cs$_{0.2}$Pb(Br$_x$I$_{1-x}$)$_3$. Photo-generated holes localize near iodide ions, triggering their oxidation and accelerating the formation of iodide vacancies, thereby promoting segregation.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes defect thermodynamics as a framework for understanding halide segregation and provides a structural basis for designing stable mixed halide perovskites.

title: First-principles identification of optically efficient erbium centers in GaAs
summary: Gallium arsenide (GaAs) doped with erbium (Er), a material of interest for optoelectronics and quantum information, has been studied for decades. Yet the formation of Er luminescence centers in the semiconductor host and their properties are still not well understood. Here we present a systematic investigation of Er-related defects in GaAs, including defect complexes consisting of Er and native point defects or oxygen impurities, using first-principles hybrid-functional defect calculations. We find that these defects have electronic structure and energetics that are generally asymmetric with respect to n- and p-type doping and tend to favor electron trapping. On the basis of the calculated defect levels, formation energies, and nonradiative carrier capture coefficients, we identify Er-related defect centers that are efficient as trap-assisted nonradiative recombination centers for Er$^{3+}$ excitation under host photoexcitation or via minority carrier injection. Our results provide an understanding for why a particular center with Er coupled to two oxygen atoms, often referred to as Er-2O, is most efficient and for the effects of n- and p-type doping and of the Er/O ratio on the formation of optically active Er centers and on the Er luminescence observed in experiments.
