AI.tldr: This study uses first-principles calculations to investigate Er-related defects in GaAs, identifying the Er-2O center as the most efficient for luminescence and explaining how doping type and Er/O ratio affect the formation of optically active Er centers.
AI.motivation: Despite decades of research, the formation mechanism and properties of Er luminescence centers in GaAs are not well understood. Understanding these defects is crucial for optoelectronics and quantum information applications.
AI.method: First-principles hybrid-functional defect calculations were performed to investigate Er-related defects in GaAs, including defect complexes consisting of Er and native point defects or oxygen impurities. The calculations examined electronic structure, energetics, formation energies, and nonradiative carrier capture coefficients.
AI.result: Er-related defects exhibit asymmetric electronic structure and energetics with respect to n- and p-type doping, favoring electron trapping. Several Er-related defect centers are identified as efficient trap-assisted nonradiative recombination centers for Er³⁺ excitation. The Er-2O center is found to be the most efficient, and its formation is influenced by doping type and Er/O ratio.
AI.conclusion: The Er-2O center (erbium coupled to two oxygen atoms) is identified as the most efficient optically active center. The study explains the effects of n- and p-type doping and Er/O ratio on the formation of Er-related luminescence centers, providing valuable guidance for optimizing Er-doped GaAs for optoelectronic and quantum information applications.

title: Melting behavior and dynamical properties of Cr2Ge2Te6 phase-change material
summary: Cr2Ge2Te6 (CrGT) is known as an intrinsic ferromagnetic semiconductor and a promising candidate for phase-change memory applications. In amorphous CrGT, Cr atoms form non-defective octahedral motifs with Te atoms, similar to those in the crystalline phase. The abundance of Cr[Te6] octahedra is regarded as the key structural factor in reducing the resistance drift coefficient of amorphous CrGT. However, the stage at which these octahedra emerge during melt-quench amorphization remains unclear. Here, we present ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations to model the melting process of crystalline CrGT and to investigate the dynamical properties of liquid and supercooled liquid CrGT in detail. Upon heating, Ge atoms are observed to leave their lattice sites earlier than Cr and Te atoms, diffusing into the van der Waals gap and initiating the collapse of the layered structure. The Cr[Te6] octahedra are more robust, maintaining their structural pattern up to 1400 K despite continuous rupture and re-formation of Cr-Te bonds. At higher temperatures, Cr and Te atoms start to migrate independently. In supercooled liquid CrGT at 550 K, most Cr-centered octahedra remain intact, with only limited Cr-Te bond breaking. The collective motion of these octahedra in this temperature regime helps explain why crystallization in CrGT devices can be accomplished in tens of nanoseconds.

AI.tldr: This study uses ab initio molecular dynamics to investigate the melting process and structural evolution of Cr2Ge2Te6 (CrGT) during melt-quench amorphization. Ge atoms diffuse first causing structural collapse, while Cr[Te6] octahedra remain robust up to 1400K and persist in the supercooled liquid state at 550K.
AI.motivation: Understanding the structural evolution of amorphous CrGT during melting is crucial for phase-change memory applications. While Cr[Te6] octahedra are known to reduce resistance drift, when and how these octahedra form during amorphization was unclear.
AI.method: Ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations were used to model the melting process of crystalline CrGT and investigate dynamical properties of liquid and supercooled liquid states.
AI.result: Ge atoms leave their lattice sites first, diffusing into the van der Waals gap and initiating structural collapse. Cr[Te6] octahedra remain robust up to 1400K despite continuous bond rupture and re-formation. In supercooled liquid at 550K, most octahedra remain intact with limited Cr-Te bond breaking.
AI.conclusion: The collective motion of intact Cr[Te6] octahedra in the supercooled liquid regime explains how crystallization in CrGT devices can be achieved in tens of nanoseconds, validating its promise for phase-change memory applications.

title: Non-Markovian heat production in ultrafast phonon dynamics
summary: High-intensity THz laser pulses enable the light-mediated control of lattice vibrations by resonantly driving selected phonon modes. On ultrafast timescales, memory effects influence the phonon dynamics and must be accounted for to describe the heat production associated with energy dissipation. Here, we establish a microscopic framework for non-Markovian phonon dynamics by deriving the noise and dissipation kernels governing a driven phonon mode. Using large-scale molecular dynamics simulations, we reconstruct these kernels directly from the many-body lattice dynamics and determine the corresponding heat production rate. Our results provide a quantitative picture of the crossover between Markovian and non-Markovian dynamics on picosecond timescales and show how the finite bandwidth of the driving field limits the dynamically relevant bath spectrum. Furthermore, we demonstrate that thermodynamic quantities such as heat production can be inferred directly from the dynamics of an individual phonon mode, enabling their experimental measurement using time-resolved spectroscopy.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a microscopic framework for non-Markovian phonon dynamics driven by high-intensity THz laser pulses, using molecular dynamics to reconstruct noise and dissipation kernels. The study reveals the picosecond-scale crossover between Markovian and non-Markovian dynamics and demonstrates that heat production can be measured experimentally via time-resolved spectroscopy by analyzing individual phonon mode dynamics.
AI.motivation: High-intensity THz laser pulses enable light-mediated control of lattice vibrations, but memory effects on ultrafast timescales influence phonon dynamics and must be accounted for to describe heat production from energy dissipation.
AI.method: Large-scale molecular dynamics simulations were used to reconstruct noise and dissipation kernels governing driven phonon modes, determining heat production rates through microscopic non-Markovian phonon dynamics framework.
AI.result: Quantitative picture of crossover between Markovian and non-Markovian dynamics on picosecond timescales; finite bandwidth of driving field limits dynamically relevant bath spectrum; thermodynamic quantities like heat production can be inferred from individual phonon mode dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables experimental measurement of heat production via time-resolved spectroscopy by inferring thermodynamic quantities from individual phonon mode dynamics, with the finite bandwidth of the driving field limiting the dynamically relevant bath spectrum.

title: Rethinking Charge Transport and Recombination in Donor-diluted Organic Solar Cells
summary: We systematically investigate PM6:Y12 bulk-heterojunction solar cells with donor fractions ranging from 1% to 45%, linking morphology, charge transport, and recombination to device performance. Complementary structural and spectroscopic methods reveal that a percolating PM6 network forms even at below 5% donor content, with lamellar stacking and vertical composition gradients that do not hinder the charge extraction. The reduction of the effective active layer conductivity towards low donor fractions obeys a three-dimensional percolation model, indicating that charge transport is governed by network topology rather without a pronounced percolation threshold. A transition from nongeminate Langevin recombination to a dispersive Smoluchowski-type loss occurs below 5% donor fraction. The latter regime is also nongeminate, i.e., pertains to recombination of the total charge carrier density. Correspondingly, we observe that the Langevin reduction in the higher donor fractions - mostly dominated by redissociation of electron-hole pairs after encounter - changes towards low donor fractions: in these cases, the nongeminate loss rate exceeds the prediction of the Langevin model. This regime coincides with increasing transport resistance due to topology-limited hole conduction, leading to reduced fill factors despite a high retained charge-generation efficiency. Our results demonstrate that strong donor dilution preserves photogeneration if a continuous donor network is maintained, and unveil how topology-controlled transport and non-Langevin recombination jointly define the performance limits of donor-diluted organic solar blends.

AI.tldr: This study investigates PM6:Y12 organic solar cells with 1-45% donor content, finding that a percolating donor network forms even at very low donor fractions. Charge transport follows 3D percolation physics, while recombination shifts from Langevin to dispersive Smoluchowski-type at low donor content, with non-Langevin losses reducing fill factors. Maintaining a continuous donor network is key to preserving photogeneration in highly diluted systems.
AI.motivation: Understanding how donor dilution affects organic solar cell performance, particularly the relationship between morphology, charge transport mechanisms, and recombination dynamics in donor-diluted systems.
AI.method: Systematic investigation of PM6:Y12 bulk-heterojunction solar cells with donor fractions from 1% to 45%, using complementary structural and spectroscopic methods to link morphology, charge transport, and recombination to device performance.
AI.result: A percolating PM6 network forms even at below 5% donor content. Charge transport is governed by network topology following a 3D percolation model without a pronounced threshold. A transition from nongeminate Langevin recombination to dispersive Smoluchowski-type loss occurs below 5% donor fraction. In low donor fractions, nongeminate loss exceeds Langevin predictions due to topology-limited hole conduction, reducing fill factors despite high charge-generation efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Strong donor dilution preserves photogeneration if a continuous donor network is maintained. Topology-controlled transport and non-Langevin recombination jointly define the performance limits of donor-diluted organic solar blends. The study provides guidance for optimizing donor-diluted organic solar cells by maintaining percolating networks while managing recombination dynamics.

title: Shape Selection in Nanopillar Formation
comment: 4 pages, 4 figures, submitted to Solid State Communications
summary: Crystal growth processes produce a diverse array of surface formations, primarily distinguished by their geometric shapes. While some structures strictly adhere to the underlying crystal symmetry, others exhibit universal circular or oval geometries. Utilizing Vicinal Cellular Automata (VicCA) modeling, we demonstrate that these morphological differences depend on the spatial distribution of the growth potential. Specifically, local potential variations concentrated around surface steps drive the formation of the lattice symmetry - following structures, whereas global potentials - often originating from defects-generate universal spherical or oval shapes. Furthermore, we illustrate how these morphologies are influenced by the growth parameters such as sticking coefficient or diffusion coefficient. Although the positioning of surface defects is difficult to control, we show that temperature and external particle flux can be effectively used to steer and manipulate surface pattern formation.

AI.tldr: This paper uses VicCA modeling to demonstrate that the morphological differences in crystal surface formations depend on the spatial distribution of growth potential - local potentials lead to lattice symmetry-following structures while global potentials produce universal spherical/oval shapes. Temperature and external particle flux can be used to control surface pattern formation.
AI.motivation: Crystal growth processes produce diverse surface formations with different geometric shapes - some follow lattice symmetry while others exhibit universal circular/oval geometries - and the underlying mechanism determining these morphological differences was not well understood.
AI.method: Vicinal Cellular Automata (VicCA) modeling was used to simulate crystal growth processes and analyze surface formation patterns.
AI.result: Local potential variations concentrated around surface steps drive the formation of lattice symmetry-following structures, while global potentials (often originating from defects) generate universal spherical oroval shapes. Growth parameters such as sticking coefficient and diffusion coefficient influence these morphologies.
AI.conclusion: Temperature and external particle flux can be effectively used to steer and manipulate surface pattern formation, offering controllable ways to influence crystal surface morphology despite the difficulty in controlling surface defect positioning.

title: Role of photonic interference in exciton-mediated magneto-optic responses
summary: Coupled optical and magnetic excitations can give rise to remarkably strong magneto-optic responses. This is particularly evident in van der Waals magnets, such as the antiferromagnet CrSBr, where excitons and magnons emerge from the same electronic orbitals. While previous work has primarily focused on uncovering the magneto-electric origin of the resulting exciton-magnon interactions, the influence of photonic effects has received comparatively little attention. Here, we use numerical simulations to disentangle exciton-magnon coupling from the exciton-mediated magnon-photon interactions observed in optical experiments. Our simulations show the strong dependence of these interactions on photonic interference and dispersion effects near excitonic resonances. Such effects shape the optical response to coherent magnons and make it intrinsically non-linear in the magnon-induced exciton energy shift. Thermal magnons, which have a particularly pronounced impact on excitons, are found to even produce qualitatively different trends in optical signatures. Depending on weak or strong coupling of excitons and photons, the same exciton-magnon interaction can lead to a red-shift of optical modes, a nearly vanishing response, or their blue-shift. Finally, we demonstrate first steps towards optimizing the multi-parameter problem of efficient magnon-photon transduction using a machine-learning approach.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the interplay between excitons, magnons, and photons in van der Waals magnet CrSBr through numerical simulations. It reveals how photonic effects and thermal magnons significantly influence magneto-optic responses, demonstrating that the same exciton-magnon interaction can produce red-shift, blue-shift, or vanishing optical responses depending on coupling conditions.
AI.motivation: While coupled optical and magnetic excitations in van der Waals magnets like CrSBr show strong magneto-optic responses with excitons and magnons emerging from same orbitals, previous research focused on magneto-electric origins of exciton-magnon interactions, leaving photonic effects largely unexplored.
AI.method: Numerical simulations to disentangle exciton-magnon coupling from exciton-mediated magnon-photon interactions, along with machine-learning optimization for efficient magnon-photon transduction.
AI.result: Strong dependence of interactions on photonic interference and dispersion near excitonic resonances; thermal magnons produce qualitatively different optical signatures; depending on weak or strong exciton-photon coupling, same exciton-magnon interaction leads to red-shift, nearly vanishing response, or blue-shift of optical modes.
AI.conclusion: Photonic effects shape optical response to coherent magnons and make it intrinsically non-linear in magnon-induced exciton energy shift. Machine-learning approach shows promise for optimizing multi-parameter magnon-photon transduction.

title: Unexpected Planar Dislocation Boundary Formation in FCC Metals Captured by Dark-Field X-ray Microscopy and Continuum Dislocation Dynamics
summary: Validating dislocation patterning models against in situ imaging experiments is a longstanding goal in materials physics. Here, we provide the first direct morphological comparison of such models. Using in situ Dark-Field X-ray Microscopy (DFXM), we map the local orientations in high-purity aluminium deformed along [100] and find unexpected planar dislocation boundaries aligned with {111} slip planes that form prior to the development of a conventional dislocation cell structure. To explain this behaviour, we generate synthetic DFXM contrast images from a continuum dislocation dynamics (CDD) simulation. This mesoscale model, using nickel as a high stacking fault energy (SFE) FCC analogue, independently predicts the formation of the same {111} planar boundary types. This correspondence demonstrates that state-of-the-art CDD and DFXM experimental data can be used synergistically - despite differences in strain rates and length scales - as a practical route for refining continuum theories of plasticity.

AI.tldr: First direct morphological comparison of dislocation patterning models against in situ imaging experiments using DFXM and CDD simulation, revealing {111} planar dislocation boundaries in aluminum that were independently predicted by the simulation.
AI.motivation: Validating dislocation patterning models against in situ imaging experiments is a longstanding goal in materials physics.
AI.method: Used in situ Dark-Field X-ray Microscopy (DFXM) to map local orientations in high-purity aluminium deformed along [100]; Generated synthetic DFXM contrast images from a continuum dislocation dynamics (CDD) simulation using nickel as a high stacking fault energy (SFE) FCC analogue.
AI.result: Discovered unexpected planar dislocation boundaries aligned with {111} slip planes that form prior to the development of a conventional dislocation cell structure. CDD simulation independently predicted the formation of the same {111} planar boundary types.
AI.conclusion: This correspondence demonstrates that state-of-the-art CDD and DFXM experimental data can be used synergistically despite differences in strain rates and length scales, providing a practical route for refining continuum theories of plasticity.

title: Strain-driven magnetic anisotropy and spin reorientation in epitaxial Co V 2 O 4 spinel oxide thin films
summary: CoV___O___ (CVO) stands out among spinel vanadates for its ultra-short V-V distances, placing it at the brink of itinerant electron behaviour-an ideal playground for strain engineering. In this work, we exploit this sensitivity by growing high-quality epitaxial CVO thin films on SrTiO___ (001) and MgO (001), inducing compressive and tensile strain, respectively. Using pulsed laser deposition under ultra-low oxygen pressure, we achieve high crystalline quality and straincontrolled tetragonal distortions: c > a under compression (STO) and c < a under tension (MgO).  Resonant elastic X-ray scattering confirms a normal spinel structure, with cobalt occupying tetrahedral sites and vanadium octahedral ones. Both strain types reduce charge transport, driving the system into a highly resistive state. Magnetic measurements reveal strain-driven anisotropy switching: STO films transition from out-of-plane to in-plane easy axis below 90 K, while MgO films flip from in-plane to out-of-plane below 45 K. These results highlight CVO's exceptional responsiveness to lattice strain, unlocking a path to finely tunable electronic and magnetic properties. With its strong spin-lattice coupling and potential in spin Hall magnetoresistance, strained CVO emerges as a compelling platform for next-generation lowpower spintronic devices.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates strain engineering in CoV2O4 (CVO) spinel thin films, demonstrating that epitaxial strain on SrTiO3 and MgO substrates can effectively tune electronic and magnetic properties, including charge transport and magnetic anisotropy switching.
AI.motivation: CVO exhibits ultra-short V-V distances placing it at the brink of itinerant electron behavior, making it highly sensitive to lattice strain - ideal for strain engineering to control electronic and magnetic properties.
AI.method: High-quality epitaxial CVO thin films were grown on SrTiO3 (001) and MgO (001) substrates using pulsed laser deposition under ultra-low oxygen pressure. Resonant elastic X-ray scattering was used to confirm the normal spinel structure.
AI.result: Compressive strain (STO: c > a) and tensile strain (MgO: c < a) both reduce charge transport, driving the system into a highly resistive state. Magnetic measurements reveal strain-driven anisotropy switching: STO films transition from out-of-plane to in-plane easy axis below 90 K, while MgO films flip from in-plane to out-of-plane below 45 K.
AI.conclusion: CVO demonstrates exceptional responsiveness to lattice strain, enabling fine tuning of electronic and magnetic properties. With strong spin-lattice coupling and potential in spin Hall magnetoresistance, strained CVO is a compelling platform for next-generation low-power spintronic devices.

title: Atomic-resolution imaging of gold species at organic liquid-solid interfaces
summary: Understanding solid-liquid interfaces at the atomic-scale is key to improved performance of heterogeneous catalysts, electrodes and membranes. Here we combine unique specimen design, record atomic resolution in situ electron microscopy, and artificial intelligence-enabled analysis to achieve a step change in quantitative understanding of interfacial atomic behaviour. We create the first graphene liquid cells with organic solvents and employ them to track over 106 gold adatoms and clusters at a graphene surface immersed in acetone and cyclohexanone. We reveal dynamic correlated behaviour of gold adatom monomers, dimers, trimers and clusters, strongly influenced by each other, the solvent properties, and the atomic lattice of the substrate, in good agreement with theoretical calculations. We use the results to interpret differences in catalytic activity towards the industrially important acetylene hydrochlorination reaction. This new capability for exploration of atomic scale chemistry could enable rational design of future catalysts, membranes and electrodes with improved functionality.

AI.tldr: First demonstration of graphene liquid cells with organic solvents enabling atomic-resolution in-situ TEM of gold adatoms and clusters at solid-liquid interfaces, revealing correlated atomic behavior influenced by solvent properties and substrate lattice, with implications for catalytic design.
AI.motivation: Understanding solid-liquid interfaces at atomic scale is critical for improving heterogeneous catalysts, electrodes, and membranes, but direct observation of interfacial atomic behavior has been technically challenging.
AI.method: Combined unique specimen design, record atomic resolution in-situ electron microscopy, and AI-enabled analysis to track over 10^6 gold adatoms and clusters at a graphene surface in acetone and cyclohexanone solvents.
AI.result: Revealed dynamic correlated behavior of gold adatom monomers, dimers, trimers and clusters, strongly influenced by each other, solvent properties, and the substrate atomic lattice, in good agreement with theoretical calculations. Results interpreted differences in catalytic activity for acetylene hydrochlorination reaction.
AI.conclusion: This new capability for atomic-scale chemistry exploration could enable rational design of future catalysts, membranes, and electrodes with improved functionality.

title: Structural phase transitions in double perovskite crystals studied by Brillouin light scattering
summary: Inorganic lead-free double perovskites represent particular interest as non-toxic and stable material platform for optoelectronic applications. Here, we employ Brillouin light scattering spectroscopy to investigate the elastic properties and structural phase transitions in single crystals of Cs2AgBiBr6 and Cs2AgBiCl6. A complete set of elastic constants is determined from the Brillouin scattering measurements performed along three different crystallographic directions. Both materials exhibit similar elastic constants and weak elastic anisotropy in the cubic phase. At low temperatures, the lifting of degeneracy of transverse acoustic phonon modes is attributed to a lowering of crystal symmetry. From the temperature dependence of the acoustic phonon frequencies, we determine the structural phase transition temperature of about 43 K for Cs2AgBiCl6, compared to 122 K for the cubic-to-tetragonal phase transition in Cs2AgBiBr6.

AI.tldr: Brillouin scattering study of lead-free double perovskites Cs2AgBiBr6 and Cs2AgBiCl6 reveals similar elastic constants with weak anisotropy in cubic phase, and identifies structural phase transitions at 43 K (Cl) and 122 K (Br).
AI.motivation: Develop non-toxic and stable lead-free perovskite materials for optoelectronic applications, while understanding their elastic properties and structural phase transitions which are critical for device performance and reliability.
AI.method: Brillouin light scattering spectroscopy was used to investigate elastic properties. Measurements were performed along three crystallographic directions to determine the complete set of elastic constants. Temperature-dependent acoustic phonon frequency measurements were conducted to study structural phase transitions.
AI.result: Both Cs2AgBiBr6 and Cs2AgBiCl6 exhibit similar elastic constants and weak elastic anisotropy in the cubic phase. At low temperatures, degeneracy lifting of transverse acoustic phonon modes occurs due to lowered crystal symmetry. The structural phase transition temperatures were determined to be ~43 K for Cs2AgBiCl6 and ~122 K for Cs2AgBiBr6 (cubic-to-tetragonal).
AI.conclusion: This study provides fundamental elastic property data for lead-free double perovskites, revealing similar elastic behavior but different phase transition temperatures between bromide and chloride variants. The findings contribute to understanding structure-property relationships for optoelectronic applications.

title: Unveiling the Thermal and Aqueous Stability of 1D Lepidocrocite Titania
summary: One dimensional lepidocrocite titanium dioxide filaments are investigated with respect to their thermal and aqueous stability. Structural and phase evolution are examined using in situ heating in vacuum within transmission electron microscopy combined with electron energy loss spectroscopy, and at ambient conditions using Raman spectroscopy. The filaments retain their lepidocrocite structure up to 300 degree and above which localized sintering and amorphization occur at filament overlap junctions. With further heating, the amorphous regions crystallize into anatase, with Raman spectroscopy corroborating the onset of structural disorder. Long term aqueous storage up to 100 days at ambient conditions induces transformation into flake like anatase nanoparticles. This process is strongly suppressed under refrigerated storage, where no structural changes are observed over the same period. These results establish critical thermal and environmental stability thresholds that define operational advantages and limits for emerging applications of 1D lepidocrocite filaments.

AI.tldr: One-dimensional lepidocrocite titanium dioxide filaments are thermally stable up to 300°C but degrade above this temperature, transforming to anatase. Aqueous storage at room temperature causes transformation to anatase nanoparticles within 100 days, but refrigerated storage prevents this transformation.
AI.motivation: The motivation was to investigate the thermal and aqueous stability of one-dimensional lepidocrocite titanium dioxide filaments, which is critical for determining their operational advantages and limits in emerging applications.
AI.method: The study employed in situ heating in vacuum within transmission electron microscopy (TEM) combined with electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS), along with Raman spectroscopy at ambient conditions. Long-term aqueous storage experiments were conducted for 100 days under both ambient and refrigerated conditions to assess environmental stability.
AI.result: The filaments retain their lepidocrocite structure up to 300°C. Above this temperature, localized sintering and amorphization occur at filament overlap junctions. With further heating, amorphous regions crystallize into anatase. Long-term aqueous storage (100 days) at ambient conditions induces transformation into flake-like anatase nanoparticles, while refrigerated storage suppresses this transformation with no structural changes observed.
AI.conclusion: This study establishes critical thermal (300°C threshold) and environmental stability thresholds for 1D lepidocrocite titanium dioxide filaments, defining their operational advantages and limits for emerging applications. Refrigerated storage is recommended to prevent aqueous transformation.

title: Second harmonic study of thermally oxidized mono- and few-layer 2H-MoS2
comment: Katharina Burgholzer and Henry Volker Hübschmann contributed equally
summary: A comprehensive study of second harmonic generation on thermally oxidized MoS2 flakes with thickness ranging from monolayer up to seven layers is presented. Observing the fundamental nonlinear behavior for non-treated and oxidized MoS2 reveals that oxidation causes significant changes in the second harmonic (SH) response for all investigated structures. Excitation power dependent measurements to analyze the nonlinear behavior with respect to the oxidation time show progressive oxidation within the maximum oxidation time of six hours, under the considered oxidation conditions. Here, polarization dependent measurements reveal the structural changes due to oxidation. Additionally, it is found that the oxidation depth is restricted to the top most layer and the oxidation behavior exhibits a layer dependency. These findings are supported by theoretical band structure calculations. The results demonstrate that the thermal oxidation progress of two dimensional MoS2 can be monitored with non-resonant and non-invasive SH microscopy, by following distinct fingerprints of structural modification in the nonlinear response.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates second harmonic generation (SHG) in thermally oxidized MoS2 flakes (monolayer to 7 layers), showing that oxidation significantly alters the SH response. Using power-dependent and polarization-dependent measurements combined with band structure calculations, the study demonstrates that SH microscopy can non-invasively monitor oxidation progress in 2D MoS2.
AI.motivation: Understanding and monitoring the oxidation behavior of 2D MoS2 is crucial for practical applications. The researchers sought to determine if SHG can serve as a non-invasive probe to track structural changes during thermal oxidation.
AI.method: The researchers performed SHG measurements on MoS2 flakes (1-7 layers) before and after thermal oxidation at different time points. They conducted excitation power-dependent measurements to analyze nonlinear behavior and polarization-dependent measurements to detect structural changes. Band structure calculations were performed to support experimental observations.
AI.result: Oxidation causes significant changes in SH response for all thicknesses studied. The oxidation depth is restricted to the topmost layer, and oxidation behavior exhibits layer dependency. Progressive oxidation was observed over up to 6 hours under the experimental conditions.
AI.conclusion: Thermal oxidation progress of 2D MoS2 can be monitored non-resonantly and non-invasively using SH microscopy by following distinct fingerprints of structural modification in the nonlinear response.

title: Defect-induced multiferroicicy in bulk solid solutions of WSe$_2$ and WTe$_2$
summary: Transition metal dichalcogenides provide a versatile platform for tunable ferroic phenomena at the atomic scale owing to their reduced dimensionality. Here we investigate the structural, magnetic, and ferroelectric properties of bulk solid solution W(Se1-xTex)2(1-delta) single crystals synthesized by chemical vapor transport. The room temperature behavior is analyzed as a function of tellurium concentration (x) and chalcogen defect fraction (delta). X ray diffraction and Raman spectroscopy reveal lattice expansion and symmetry reduction with increasing x, consistent with a 2H to 1Td structural transition above a critical composition xc about 18 percent. Piezoresponse force microscopy identifies piezoelectricity near stoichiometric compositions (delta less than 5 percent) and switchable ferroelectricity in the chalcogen deficient regime (delta greater than 20 percent). Magnetometry measurements show a corresponding evolution from paramagnetic to ferromagnetic behavior with increasing delta. Near stoichiometric Te poor samples exhibit piezoelectric and paramagnetic responses, whereas multiferroic states characterized by the coexistence of ferroelectric and ferromagnetic responses emerge at high vacancy concentrations. The performed characterizations indicate that x primarily governs structural symmetry, while delta controls the emergence of both ferromagnetic and ferroelectric responses. These trends are summarized in a configurational phase diagram highlighting the cooperative influence of dopants and defects on ferroic behavior. Overall, controlled stoichiometry and vacancy engineering offer an effective strategy to tailor ferroic responses in transition metal dichalcogenides.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates structural, magnetic, and ferroelectric properties of bulk solid solution W(Se1-xTex)2(1-delta) single crystals, demonstrating that tellurium concentration (x) controls structural symmetry while chalcogen defect fraction (delta) governs emergence of ferromagnetic and ferroelectric responses, enabling multiferroic behavior at high vacancy concentrations.
AI.motivation: Transition metal dichalcogenides offer a versatile platform for tunable ferroic phenomena at the atomic scale due to reduced dimensionality, but the ability to precisely control and engineer these properties through composition and defects remains a key challenge.
AI.method: Single crystals of W(Se1-xTex)2(1-delta) were synthesized by chemical vapor transport. Properties were characterized using X-ray diffraction (structural analysis), Raman spectroscopy (symmetry analysis), piezoresponse force microscopy (piezoelectricity and ferroelectricity), and magnetometry (magnetic behavior).
AI.result: A 2H to 1Td structural transition occurs above critical composition xc ≈ 18% Te. Piezoelectricity exists near stoichiometric compositions (delta < 5%), while switchable ferroelectricity emerges in chalcogen-deficient regime (delta > 20%). Paramagnetic behavior evolves to ferromagnetic behavior with increasing delta. Near-stoichiometric Te-poor samples show piezoelectric + paramagnetic responses, while high vacancy concentrations yield multiferroic states with coexisting ferroelectric and ferromagnetic responses.
AI.conclusion: Tellurium concentration (x) primarily governs structural symmetry, while chalcogen defect fraction (delta) controls both ferromagnetic and ferroelectric responses. A configurational phase diagram was developed showing cooperative influence of dopants and defects. Controlled stoichiometry and vacancy engineering provide an effective strategy to tailor ferroic responses in transition metal dichalcogenides.

title: Anisotropic implantation damage build-up and crystal recovery in $β$-Ga$_2$O$_3$
summary: The present work aims at investigating the defect accumulation and recovery dynamics in the inherently anisotropic $β$-Ga$_2$O$_3$ lattice. A systematic Rutherford Backscattering Spectrometry in Channelling mode (RBS/C) analysis of Cr-implanted samples was performed across multiple surface orientations and channelling directions. Distinct apparent defect accumulation and annealing rates were observed along different channelling axes, mainly attributed to the shadowing of certain types of defects along some directions. The efficient defect removal observed after annealing was correlated with the strain relaxation observed via High-Resolution X-ray diffraction (HRXRD) at temperatures as low as 500 °C, which is attributed to the removal of point defects. Annealing at higher temperature further improves crystalline quality but at a slower rate. In short, this work enhances the understanding of the effect of structural anisotropic properties of $β$-Ga$_2$O$_3$ during ion implantation, as well as the crystal recovery during thermal annealing, highlighting the interplay between crystallography and defect dynamics.

AI.tldr: This work investigates defect accumulation and recovery dynamics in β-Ga2O3 using RBS/C and HRXRD. Cr-implanted samples show anisotropic defect behavior along different channelling axes, with efficient defect removal at 500°C correlated with strain relaxation.
AI.motivation: Understand the effect of structural anisotropic properties of β-Ga2O3 during ion implantation and crystal recovery during thermal annealing.
AI.method: RBS/C analysis of Cr-implanted samples across multiple surface orientations and channelling directions; HRXRD for strain analysis.
AI.result: Distinct apparent defect accumulation and annealing rates were observed along different channelling axes due to defect shadowing. Efficient defect removal after annealing was correlated with strain relaxation at temperatures as low as 500°C, with further improvement at higher temperatures but at slower rate.
AI.conclusion: This work enhances understanding of the interplay between crystallography and defect dynamics in β-Ga2O3 during ion implantation and thermal annealing.

title: Au and Ag nanoparticles produced by ion implantation in single-crystalline $β$-Ga$_2$O$_3$
summary: This work reports the successful formation of Ag and Au nanoparticles in $β$-Ga$_2$O$_3$ single-crystals by ion implantation and annealing at 550 °C. X-ray diffraction measurements revealed that nanoparticles were formed after the annealing step, presenting a highly-ordered crystalline structure and conforming to a crystallographic relation with respect to the matrix: $\left(0\overline{1}0\right)_β\parallel \left(110\right)_{\mathrm{Ag/Au}}$ and $\left[102\right]_β\parallel \left[1\overline{1}2\right]_{\mathrm{Ag/Au}}$. The presence of these nanoparticles was also confirmed via absorbance measurements revealing the localised surface plasmon resonance peaks associated with these particles. Considering the multiple advantages and the versatility of metallic nanoparticles, their combination with the exceptional properties of $β$-Ga$_2$O$_3$ paves the way for a wide range of applications.

AI.tldr: Ag and Au nanoparticles successfully formed in β-Ga₂O₃ single crystals via ion implantation and 550°C annealing, showing crystallographic alignment and surface plasmon resonance.
AI.motivation: The research aims to combine the versatile properties of metallic nanoparticles (Ag, Au) with the exceptional properties of β-Ga₂O₃ semiconductor to enable various applications.
AI.method: Ion implantation technique followed by annealing at 550 °C was used to form metallic nanoparticles within β-Ga₂O₃ single-crystal matrix.
AI.result: X-ray diffraction confirmed formation of highly-ordered crystalline nanoparticles with specific crystallographic relations: (0̅10)_β || (110)_Ag/Au and [102]_β || [1̅12]_Ag/Au. Localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) peaks were detected via absorbance measurements, confirming nanoparticle presence.
AI.conclusion: The successful integration of Ag and Au nanoparticles with β-Ga₂O₃ creates promising opportunities for wide-ranging applications leveraging the combined properties of metallic nanoparticles and the wide-bandgap semiconductor.
